> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT636 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Current Source Inverters; 180° and 120° Modes; PWM Inverters; AC-AC Converters: Single-Phase and Three-Phase AC-AC Converters; Cycloconverters (Single-Phase)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Current Source Inverters (CSI)

### Formal KTU Definition
A **Current Source Inverter (CSI)** is a power electronic converter that inverts a DC input current into an AC output current of variable frequency. Unlike a Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) which maintains a constant DC link voltage, the CSI is fed from a **stiff DC current source** (typically realized using a large inductor L in series with a DC source) and feeds a **constant-magnitude current waveform** to the load, the shape of which depends on the switching sequence of the thyristors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The CSI uses **thyristors (SCRs)** as the switching devices because the commutation circuit relies on the load or a commutation capacitor to force-commutate the devices — thyristors cannot self-turn-off.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of a **garden hose with a piston pump**:
- The pump (DC source) pushes a **constant flow of water** (constant current) through the hose.
- The valve system (thyristor bridge) **redirects** the direction of water flow back and forth at controlled intervals, producing an alternating output.
- The **flow magnitude stays fixed** (stiff current source), but the **direction alternates** — just like a CSI.

| Feature | VSI | CSI |
|---|---|---|
| DC Source | Voltage Source (Capacitor) | Current Source (Inductor) |
| Output quantity controlled | Voltage | Current |
| Switching device | IGBT / MOSFET / BJT | Thyristor (SCR) |
| Commutation | Device commutation (self) | Load or forced commutation |
| Typical application | PWM drives, UPS | Induction heating, synchronous motor drives |

---

## 1.2 180° and 120° Conduction Modes (VSI Operation)

### 180° Conduction Mode
In the **180° conduction mode** of a three-phase VSI, **each thyristor (or switching device) conducts for 180° of the output cycle**. At any instant, **three devices conduct simultaneously** — one from the positive half (T1, T3, T5) and one from the negative half (T2, T4, T6), totalling 3 devices ON. The conduction sequence is:
$$T_1 \rightarrow T_2 \rightarrow T_3 \rightarrow T_4 \rightarrow T_5 \rightarrow T_6 \rightarrow T_1$$
Each device is ON for a half-cycle (180°).

### 120° Conduction Mode
In the **120° conduction mode**, **each device conducts for 120°** of the cycle. At any instant, only **two devices conduct simultaneously** (one from upper group, one from lower group), with a 60° gap between the turn-off of one device and the turn-on of the next in the same leg. This mode provides better reliability because it avoids **shoot-through faults** (cross-conduction in the same leg).

### Real-World Analogy
- **180° mode** = "Relays always closed" — three relays work simultaneously like a tag-team relay race where every baton carrier is running.
- **120° mode** = "Two at a time" — only two clerks are open at a service counter, with brief idle gaps.

---

## 1.3 PWM Inverters

### Definition
A **Pulse Width Modulated (PWM) Inverter** is a voltage source inverter in which the DC input is chopped into a series of pulses of varying widths within each half-cycle to **synthesize a sinusoidal output**. The fundamental component is enhanced while the lower-order harmonics are reduced or eliminated.

> [!NOTE]
> PWM techniques include: **Single-Pulse Modulation, Multiple-Pulse Modulation, Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM), Modified SPWM (with 3rd harmonic injection), and Space Vector PWM (SVPWM)**. KTU focuses on SPWM and Multiple-Pulse Modulation.

### Analogy
Imagine blowing air through a **reed instrument** — instead of blowing continuously, you pulse the air in **varying intensities** such that the *average airflow* over time mimics a smooth sine wave. PWM does the same with voltage — varying pulse widths to recreate a sinusoid.

---

## 1.4 AC-AC Voltage Converters

### Definition
An **AC-AC Voltage Converter** (also called an **AC Voltage Controller** or **AC Chopper**) is a converter that converts a fixed AC voltage (fixed magnitude and fixed frequency) into a **variable AC voltage** of the **same frequency**, using line-commutated thyristors. No intermediate DC link is involved.

| Configuration | Application |
|---|---|
| **Single-Phase AC-AC Converter** | Domestic fan regulators, lamp dimmers, heater control |
| **Three-Phase AC-AC Converter** | Industrial fan drives, pump drives, soft starters |

### Analogy
A **dimmer switch** in your home is essentially a single-phase AC-AC voltage controller — by adjusting the firing angle α of a TRIAC, you control how much of each half-cycle is delivered to the lamp.

---

## 1.5 Cycloconverters

### Definition
A **Cycloconverter** is an **AC-AC frequency changer** that converts AC power from one frequency to another **without an intermediate DC link**. It uses **naturally-commutated thyristor bridges** to synthesize the lower-frequency output by selecting appropriate portions of the input AC waveform.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Two main types:
> - **Step-down Cycloconverter**: Output frequency $f_o < f_i$ (input frequency). Common in **low-speed high-power AC drives**.
> - **Step-up Cycloconverter**: Output frequency $f_o > f_i$. Less common; uses forced commutation.

### Analogy
Think of **time-lapse photography of waves**: you're capturing the slow rise and fall of ocean waves (low frequency) by sampling frames from a fast-shutter video (high frequency input). The cycloconverter "samples" the input AC waveform to construct a slower (or different frequency) AC output.

### Block-Level Comparison Summary
```
+---------------------+       +--------------------+
|  AC INPUT           |       |  DC INPUT          |
| (Fixed f, V)        |       | (Fixed V)          |
+----------+----------+       +----------+---------+
           |                             |
           v                             v
   +---------------+             +---------------+
   | AC-AC Converter|             |  INVERTER (VSI/CSI)
   | (Same freq)    |             | (Variable f)   |
   +-------+-------+             +-------+-------+
           |                             |
           v                             v
    Variable V, Same f              Variable V, f
    (Dimmer/Regulator)             (Motor Drive)
```

```
+---------------------+       +--------------------+
|  AC INPUT (f_i)     |       |  AC INPUT (f_i)    |
+----------+----------+       +----------+---------+
           |                             |
           v                             v
   +---------------+             +---------------+
   | CYCLOCONVERTER|             | RECTIFIER      |
   | (f_o != f_i)   |            | (AC -> DC)     |
   +-------+-------+             +-------+-------+
           |                             |
           v                             v
    Variable f AC                DC LINK + INVERTER
   (Direct freq change)         (Indirect, with DC link)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Current Source Inverter — Operating Principle

### Circuit Topology
A single-phase **Auto-Sequential Commutated CSI** (most commonly used with thyristors) uses:
- A **large series inductor L** at the DC source (creates stiff current source)
- **Thyristor switches** T1–T4 arranged in an H-bridge
- **Commutation capacitors** C1–C4 to force-commutate the thyristors

### Operating Sequence
| Step | Time | Action |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $0$ – $180°$ | T1, T2 ON; load current flows in positive direction ($+I_d$) |
| 2 | $180°$ – $360°$ | T3, T4 ON; load current flows in negative direction ($-I_d$) |

The **load current waveform** is a **square wave** of amplitude $I_d$ regardless of load impedance.

### Why Use CSI for Induction Heating?
In **induction heating**, the load is essentially a transformer with a series-resonant LC circuit. CSI provides a **constant current**, ensuring **stable power delivery** even as the load parameters (R, L) vary with temperature.

---

## 2.2 180° Conduction Mode — Detailed Logic

For a three-phase VSI in 180° mode:

- **Conduction sequence (each 60°):**
$$T_1T_6 \rightarrow T_1T_2 \rightarrow T_2T_3 \rightarrow T_3T_4 \rightarrow T_4T_5 \rightarrow T_5T_6$$

- **Number of devices conducting at any instant = 3** (two from one group + one from the other, but always three legs active).

- **Line-to-line voltages** are 6-step waveforms with peak value $\pm V_s$ (DC link voltage).

### Waveform Properties
- **Phase voltages** have a peak of $\frac{2V_s}{3}$ and contain **triplen harmonics** (3rd, 9th, 15th, …).
- **Line voltages** do NOT contain triplen harmonics (they cancel in line-to-line difference).

---

## 2.3 120° Conduction Mode — Detailed Logic

For a three-phase VSI in 120° mode:

- **Conduction sequence (each 60°):**
$$T_1T_6 \rightarrow T_1T_2 \rightarrow T_2T_3 \rightarrow T_3T_4 \rightarrow T_4T_5 \rightarrow T_5T_6$$
(Same pair sequence as 180°, but **each device is ON only 120°**, not 180°.)

- **Number of devices conducting at any instant = 2**.
- There is a **60° dead band** in each phase where no device in that leg is conducting.

### Comparison Table
| Parameter | 180° Mode | 120° Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Conduction period per device | $180°$ | $120°$ |
| Devices ON simultaneously | 3 | 2 |
| Switching losses | Higher | Lower |
| Reliability (shoot-through risk) | Lower (no dead time available) | Higher (60° margin) |
| Output voltage utilization | Better | Slightly lower |

---

## 2.4 PWM Techniques — Stepwise Logic

### Single-Pulse PWM
- One pulse per half-cycle.
- Pulse width $2d$ is varied to control output.
- **Width $2d$** is centred in the half-cycle of width $\pi$.

### Multiple-Pulse PWM
- **N pulses per half-cycle** of equal width.
- Reduces low-order harmonics significantly.
- Pulse width $2d$ and number of pulses $N$ can be chosen to eliminate a specific harmonic.

### Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)
- A **reference sine wave** $v_r = V_r \sin(\omega t)$ is compared with a **high-frequency triangular carrier** $v_c$ of frequency $f_c$.
- **Modulation index**:
$$M = \frac{V_r}{V_c}$$
- The intersections of sine and triangle define the switching instants.

### Harmonic Elimination PWM (Selected Harmonic Elimination — SHE)
Choose the switching angles $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, …, \alpha_N$ such that:
- **Fundamental amplitude = desired value**
- **Specific lower-order harmonics = zero**

The Fourier series of a quarter-wave symmetric waveform:
$$b_n = \frac{4V_s}{n\pi} \left[ \sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^{k+1} \cos(n\alpha_k) \right]$$

For **N=3**, eliminating 5th and 7th harmonics, the transcendental equations must be solved numerically.

---

## 2.5 Single-Phase AC-AC Voltage Controller

### Circuit
- Two **back-to-back connected thyristors** (or a single TRIAC) in series with the load.
- For an AC input $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, the thyristors are fired at angle $\alpha$ in each half-cycle.

### RMS Output Voltage Derivation
For the conducting interval $[\alpha, \pi]$ (positive half-cycle) and $[\pi+\alpha, 2\pi]$ (negative half-cycle):

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$$

where $V_s = V_m / \sqrt{2}$ is the RMS source voltage.

### Power Factor
$$PF = \frac{V_{o,\text{rms}}}{V_s} = \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$$

The input current is **non-sinusoidal**, so the true PF also includes the **distortion factor**, which decreases as $\alpha$ increases.

### Modes of Operation
1. **ON-OFF Control** (Integral Cycle Control): thyristors are turned ON for $n$ cycles and OFF for $m$ cycles.
2. **Phase-Angle Control**: varying firing angle $\alpha$ continuously.
3. **Pulse Width Modulation AC Chopper**: using fully-controlled switches (IGBTs) for high-frequency chopping of AC waveform.

---

## 2.6 Three-Phase AC-AC Voltage Controller

### Three Common Configurations
| Config | Description |
|---|---|
| **3-Φ Fully Controlled** | 6 thyristors, 2 in each phase (back-to-back). Most flexible. |
| **3-Φ Half Controlled** | 3 thyristors + 3 diodes. Asymmetric; cheaper but limited control. |
| **3-Φ Delta-Connected** | Thyristors in delta branches; circulating currents possible. |

### Conduction Modes
- For a **star-connected load with isolated neutral**, at least **two phases must conduct** at any time.
- **Mode I**: $60° \leq \alpha \leq 90°$ — 2 devices conduct.
- **Mode II**: $90° \leq \alpha \leq 150°$ — 3 or 4 devices conduct (transitional).

---

## 2.7 Cycloconverter — Operating Theory

### Step-Down Cycloconverter
- **Two anti-parallel thyristor bridges** per phase (P-bridge and N-bridge).
- P-bridge operates during **positive half-cycle** of output.
- N-bridge operates during **negative half-cycle** of output.
- Each bridge is **phase-controlled** such that the average output follows a sinusoidal reference.

### Output Frequency Constraint
$$f_o = \frac{1}{T_o}, \quad f_o < f_i$$
For a clean output, the **highest output frequency** is limited by:
$$f_{o,\max} \approx \frac{f_i}{3} \text{ to } \frac{f_i}{2}$$
Beyond this, the **subharmonics** and the **distortion** become unacceptable.

### Output Voltage Equation
For a single-phase cycloconverter with P-bridge:
$$v_o = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha - \frac{4V_m}{3\pi} \cos(3\alpha)\cos(3\omega t) + \cdots$$
where $\alpha$ is the firing angle.

The fundamental component:
$$v_{o1}(t) = V_{o1} \sin(\omega_o t) = \frac{2\sqrt{2} V_s}{\pi} \sin(\omega_o t)$$

For the P-bridge, $\alpha = \frac{\pi}{2} - \omega_o t$, and for N-bridge, $\alpha = \frac{3\pi}{2} - \omega_o t$.

---

## 2.8 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$ | AC-AC controller RMS output | $\alpha$ in rad |
| 2 | $PF = \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$ | Displacement + Distortion PF | Single phase |
| 3 | $V_{o1} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \sin(d)$ | Fundamental of single-pulse PWM | V (rms) |
| 4 | $V_{o1} = \frac{4V_s}{\pi} \left[\sum_{k=1}^{N} (-1)^{k+1} \sin(n\omega t_k)\right]$ | Multiple-pulse PWM general | Fourier |
| 5 | $M = \frac{V_r}{V_c}$ | Modulation index | $0 \leq M \leq 1$ |
| 6 | $V_{LL,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}} V_s$ | Line voltage RMS (VSI 180°) | $V_s$ = DC link |
| 7 | $V_{ph,\text{rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{3} V_s$ | Phase voltage RMS (VSI 180°) | Same |
| 8 | $V_{o1} = \frac{2\sqrt{2} V_s}{\pi}$ | Square-wave VSI fundamental | per phase |
| 9 | $\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_n^2}}{V_1}$ | Total Harmonic Distortion | unitless |
| 10 | $f_{o,\max} \approx \frac{f_i}{2}$ | Cycloconverter output limit | step-down |
| 11 | $V_{LL1} = \frac{2\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_s$ | Line-line fundamental (VSI 180°) | $V_s$ = DC link |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: PWM inverters are the backbone of modern **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** used in HVAC, elevators, electric vehicles, and renewable energy inverters. AC-AC converters dominate **lighting and heating controls**. Cycloconverters are indispensable for **very high-power low-speed drives** such as cement mills, rolling mills, and ship propulsion.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage of Single-Phase AC-AC Voltage Controller

### Given
- Source: $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$
- Thyristors fired at $\alpha$ in each half-cycle
- Load: Resistive R

### Step 1: Define the Output Voltage in Positive Half-Cycle
$$v_o(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & 0 \leq \omega t < \alpha \\ V_m \sin(\omega t), & \alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi \end{cases}$$

By symmetry, the same waveform repeats in the negative half-cycle.

### Step 2: Write the RMS Formula
The RMS value over a complete cycle $T = 2\pi/\omega$ is:
$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{0}^{2\pi} v_o^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

### Step 3: Use Symmetry to Reduce Integral
By half-wave symmetry, we only integrate over $[\alpha, \pi]$ and multiply by 2:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{2}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)$$

### Step 4: Apply the Half-Angle Identity
Use $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} \, d(\omega t)$$

### Step 5: Integrate Each Term
$$= \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\omega t)\Big|_{\alpha}^{\pi} - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\Big|_{\alpha}^{\pi} \right]$$

### Step 6: Evaluate the Limits
For the first term:
$$(\omega t)\Big|_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \pi - \alpha$$

For the second term:
$$\frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2}\Big|_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{\sin(2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} = \frac{0 - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} = -\frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}$$

### Step 7: Substitute Back
$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]$$

### Step 8: Simplify in Terms of Source RMS
Since $V_s = V_m / \sqrt{2}$, we have $V_m^2 = 2V_s^2$:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{2V_s^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right] = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi}\left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]$$

### Step 9: Final Result

$$
V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of Limits:**
> - At $\alpha = 0$ (full conduction): $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - 0 + 0} = V_s$ ✓ (full output)
> - At $\alpha = \pi$ (no conduction): $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - 1 + 0} = 0$ ✓ (no output)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Fundamental Output Voltage of a Three-Phase 180° VSI

### Step 1: Define Phase Voltage Waveform (Phase A)
For 180° mode, the phase voltage $v_{AN}$ is a stepped waveform:
- $v_{AN} = +\frac{2V_s}{3}$ when T1 is ON (with T6, T2 active)
- $v_{AN} = -\frac{V_s}{3}$ otherwise (with one device from upper, two from lower active)

### Step 2: Express as Periodic Step Function
The waveform has **period $T = 2\pi/\omega$** and is **odd-symmetric** with **quarter-wave symmetry**.

### Step 3: Write Fourier Series
For an odd function with quarter-wave symmetry, only **odd sine harmonics** exist:

$$v_{AN}(t) = \sum_{n=1,3,5,...}^{\infty} b_n \sin(n\omega t)$$

$$b_n = \frac{4}{T} \int_{0}^{T/4} v_{AN}(t) \sin(n\omega t) \, dt$$

### Step 4: Apply to Stepped Waveform
Using the piecewise values and integrating:

$$b_n = \frac{4}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{0}^{\pi/3} \frac{2V_s}{3} \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t) + \int_{\pi/3}^{\pi/2} \left(-\frac{V_s}{3}\right) \sin(n\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \right]$$

### Step 5: Evaluate Integrals
First integral:
$$\int_{0}^{\pi/3} \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta = \frac{1 - \cos(n\pi/3)}{n}$$

Second integral:
$$\int_{\pi/3}^{\pi/2} \sin(n\theta) \, d\theta = \frac{\cos(n\pi/3) - \cos(n\pi/2)}{n}$$

### Step 6: Combine
$$b_n = \frac{2V_s}{3\pi n} \left[ 2(1 - \cos(n\pi/3)) - (\cos(n\pi/3) - \cos(n\pi/2)) \right]$$

$$b_n = \frac{2V_s}{3\pi n} \left[ 2 - 3\cos(n\pi/3) + \cos(n\pi/2) \right]$$

### Step 7: Fundamental Component ($n = 1$)

$$b_1 = \frac{2V_s}{3\pi} \left[ 2 - 3\cos(\pi/3) + \cos(\pi/2) \right]$$

Since $\cos(\pi/3) = 0.5$ and $\cos(\pi/2) = 0$:

$$b_1 = \frac{2V_s}{3\pi} \left[ 2 - 1.5 + 0 \right] = \frac{2V_s}{3\pi} (0.5) = \frac{V_s}{3\pi} \cdot 2$$

Wait, let me recompute carefully:
$$b_1 = \frac{2V_s}{3\pi \cdot 1}[2 - 1.5 + 0] = \frac{2V_s}{3\pi} \cdot 0.5 = \frac{V_s}{3\pi}$$

### Step 8: RMS Value of Fundamental

$$
V_{AN1,\text{rms}} = \frac{b_1}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{V_s}{3\pi\sqrt{2}} \cdot 2 = \frac{\sqrt{2} V_s}{3\pi}
$$

Numerically: $V_{AN1,\text{rms}} \approx 0.15 V_s$ — but this is per-phase. The line-to-line fundamental is:

$$
V_{LL1,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{3} \cdot V_{AN1,\text{rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{6} V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.7799 \, V_s
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: SPWM Output — Modulation Index Relationship

### Step 1: SPWM Modulation Index
For **bipolar SPWM** with modulation index $M$ (where $0 \leq M \leq 1$):

$$v_o(t) = M \cdot V_s \cdot \sin(\omega t)$$

### Step 2: Peak Output Voltage
$$V_{o,\text{peak}} = M \cdot V_s$$

### Step 3: RMS Output Voltage
$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = \frac{M \cdot V_s}{\sqrt{2}}$$

### Step 4: Maximum Linear Modulation
At $M = 1$, the output peak equals $V_s$, but **overmodulation** ($M > 1$) introduces distortion. In the linear region, **the output voltage is directly proportional to M**.

### Step 5: Third-Harmonic Injection Technique
By adding a **3rd harmonic component** (of appropriate magnitude, typically $\approx 1/6$ of fundamental) to the reference sine, the linear modulation range is extended by ~15.5%:

$$v_r(t) = V_r \sin(\omega t) + k \cdot V_r \sin(3\omega t)$$

The optimum injection coefficient: $k = 1/6$, giving maximum modulation index $M_{max} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Cycloconverter Output for Step-Down Operation

### Given
- Input: $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega_i t)$, with $f_i = \omega_i / 2\pi$
- Output: $v_o(t) = V_{mo} \sin(\omega_o t)$, with $f_o < f_i$
- Single-phase half-wave bridge cycloconverter

### Step 1: P-Bridge Firing Scheme
The firing angle of P-bridge is modulated as:
$$\alpha_P(\omega_o t) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \omega_o t \quad \text{(radians)}$$

This makes the fundamental component:
$$v_{o,P}(t) = V_{mo} \sin(\omega_o t)$$

### Step 2: Fundamental Amplitude
The fundamental of a phase-controlled bridge output is:
$$V_{o1} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ph} \cos\alpha$$

where $V_{ph}$ is the RMS phase voltage of the input AC.

### Step 3: Substituting $\alpha_P$
$$V_{o1} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ph} \cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \omega_o t\right) = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ph} \sin(\omega_o t)$$

### Step 4: Average Output Per Half-Cycle
The instantaneous average of the cycloconverter output (over one input cycle) equals:

$$v_o(t) = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ph} \sin(\omega_o t) = V_{mo} \sin(\omega_o t)$$

Hence:
$$V_{mo} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_{ph}$$

### Step 5: Subharmonic Limitation
The maximum output frequency without subharmonics:
$$f_{o,\max} = \frac{f_i}{3} \text{ (for 6-pulse bridge, clean output)}$$
$$f_{o,\max} = \frac{f_i}{2} \text{ (acceptable distortion for some applications)}$$

### Step 6: Result Summary
For a **single-phase, full-bridge, 2-pulse cycloconverter** with input voltage $V_s$ (rms):

$$
\boxed{V_{o,\text{fund,rms}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_s \approx 0.9 \, V_s}
$$

For a **three-pulse cycloconverter**:
$$V_{o,\text{fund,rms}} = \frac{\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_s \approx 0.78 \, V_s$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — AC-AC Converter with R Load

> **Problem:** A single-phase AC voltage controller has $V_s = 230$ V (rms), $f = 50$ Hz, $R = 10$ Ω. Calculate $V_{o,\text{rms}}$, $I_{o,\text{rms}}$, and PF for $\alpha = 60° = \pi/3$ rad.

### Step 1: Calculate $V_{o,\text{rms}}$
$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = 230 \sqrt{1 - \frac{\pi/3}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\pi/3)}{2\pi}}$$

$$= 230 \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{3} + \frac{\sin(120°)}{2\pi}}$$

$$= 230 \sqrt{1 - 0.3333 + \frac{0.8660}{6.2832}}$$

$$= 230 \sqrt{0.6667 + 0.1378}$$

$$= 230 \sqrt{0.8045} = 230 \times 0.8970 = 206.3 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2: Calculate $I_{o,\text{rms}}$
$$I_{o,\text{rms}} = \frac{V_{o,\text{rms}}}{R} = \frac{206.3}{10} = 20.63 \text{ A}$$

### Step 3: Calculate Power Factor
$$PF = \sqrt{0.8045} = 0.897 \text{ (lagging)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** At $\alpha = 60°$, the AC-AC controller delivers about **80.45% of the source voltage** to the load, with a power factor of 0.897. As $\alpha$ increases, both $V_{o,\text{rms}}$ and PF decrease, but PF drops faster due to increased waveform distortion.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | CSI | VSI | AC-AC Controller | Cycloconverter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Input** | DC current | DC voltage | AC voltage (fixed f) | AC voltage (fixed f) |
| **Output** | AC current (variable f) | AC voltage (variable f) | AC voltage (variable V, fixed f) | AC voltage (variable f) |
| **Switching Device** | Thyristor (with forced commutation) | IGBT / Thyristor | TRIAC / back-to-back SCRs | Thyristor (line-commutated) |
| **Harmonic Content** | Depends on switching | Depends on PWM | Poor (high distortion at high α) | Moderate; subharmonics |
| **Efficiency** | Medium | High (with PWM) | Lower at high α | Medium |
| **Application** | Induction heating, large synchronous drives | Motor drives, UPS, solar | Dimmer, soft starter, heater | Cement mills, ship propulsion |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** 
> **Compare Voltage Source Inverter (VSI) and Current Source Inverter (CSI) in terms of (i) DC input, (ii) switching device used, and (iii) output waveform controlled.**

### Model Answer

| Feature | VSI | CSI |
|---|---|---|
| DC Input | Stiff DC voltage (large filter capacitor) | Stiff DC current (large series inductor) |
| Switching Device | IGBT / MOSFET / BJT / Thyristor (with self-commutation) | Thyristor (with forced or load commutation) |
| Output Controlled | Output voltage waveform | Output current waveform |
| Load Type | Sensitive to load type | Insensitive to load type |

> **Conclusion:** CSI is preferred where the load requires a constant current and where the input supply can support an inductive DC link (e.g., induction heating, large synchronous motor drives).

---

### Question A2
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **What is a Cycloconverter? Mention two applications.**

### Model Answer
A **Cycloconverter** is a static power frequency changer that converts AC power from one frequency to another (typically a lower frequency) **without an intermediate DC link**, by controlling the firing angles of line-commutated thyristor bridges.

**Two Applications:**
1. **Slow-speed high-power AC drives** — e.g., cement mills, rolling mills, mine hoists, ship propellers.
2. **Induction heating** at variable low frequencies from a 50/60 Hz mains supply.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided)

### QUESTION A — 14 MARKS

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 3]**
> **(a)** Explain the operation of a **single-phase full-bridge Current Source Inverter (CSI)** with a neat circuit diagram and waveform. Discuss the role of commutation capacitors. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **RMS output voltage of a single-phase AC voltage controller** feeding a resistive load. Show that $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a): Single-Phase Full-Bridge CSI — Operation

**Circuit Description:**
- DC source $V_d$ in series with large inductor $L_d$ forms a **stiff DC current source** $I_d$.
- Four thyristors T1, T2, T3, T4 form an H-bridge.
- Four commutation capacitors C1, C2, C3, C4 (one across each thyristor) provide **forced commutation**.

**Mode 1 (0° to 180° of output):**
- T1 and T2 are fired. Load current = $+I_d$.
- T1 and T2 conduct; T3 and T4 are OFF.
- Commutation capacitors C1 and C2 are pre-charged to enable the next commutation.

**Mode 2 (180° to 360° of output):**
- T3 and T4 are fired. The capacitors C1 and C2 apply a reverse voltage across T1 and T2, **force-commutating** them.
- Load current = $-I_d$.

**Role of Commutation Capacitors:**
- They **store energy** to apply a reverse bias across the conducting thyristor.
- They ensure the thyristor's turn-off time $t_q$ is respected (current must remain below holding and reverse-biased for $t_q$).
- Without them, **thyristors cannot be turned off** (since they are naturally-commutated devices and the load is DC-current-fed).

**Waveform:** The output current is a **square wave** of amplitude $\pm I_d$, regardless of load.

---

#### Part (b): RMS Output Derivation (Full Derivation Repeated for Examination)

For an AC-AC voltage controller feeding a resistive load with firing angle $\alpha$:

The output voltage $v_o(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $\alpha \leq \omega t \leq \pi$ in each half-cycle, and zero elsewhere.

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) \times 2$$

$$= \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} d(\omega t)$$

$$= \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]$$

Substituting $V_m^2 = 2V_s^2$:

$$V_{o,\text{rms}}^2 = \frac{V_s^2}{\pi}\left[(\pi - \alpha) + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2}\right]$$

$$V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}}$$

**Verification:** $\alpha = 0 \Rightarrow V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s$; $\alpha = \pi \Rightarrow V_{o,\text{rms}} = 0$. ✓

---

### QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 3]**
> **(a)** With the help of **conduction diagrams**, explain the operation of a three-phase VSI operating in **180° mode**. Show the conduction sequence for one complete cycle. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain the principle of operation of a **single-phase cycloconverter**. Derive the expression for the **fundamental output voltage**. State the limitation on output frequency. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a): Three-Phase VSI — 180° Conduction Mode

**Conduction Sequence (60° intervals):**

| Interval | Devices ON | Phase A | Phase B | Phase C |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0°–60° | T1, T6, T5 | +$V_s$/2 | −$V_s$/2 | 0 |
| 60°–120° | T1, T2, T6 | +$V_s$/2 | 0 | −$V_s$/2 |
| 120°–180° | T1, T2, T3 | 0 | +$V_s$/2 | −$V_s$/2 |
| 180°–240° | T2, T3, T4 | −$V_s$/2 | +$V_s$/2 | 0 |
| 240°–300° | T3, T4, T5 | −$V_s$/2 | 0 | +$V_s$/2 |
| 300°–360° | T4, T5, T6 | 0 | −$V_s$/2 | +$V_s$/2 |

**Key Observations:**
1. **Each thyristor conducts for 180°** continuously — hence the name.
2. **Three thyristors conduct at any instant** — one upper group + two lower (or vice versa).
3. The phase voltage waveform is a **6-step quasi-square wave** with peak $\frac{2V_s}{3}$.
4. The line voltage is also 6-stepped with peak $V_s$.
5. **No dead time** required; risk of **shoot-through fault** is higher if device turn-off times are not respected.

**Phase Voltages (Fourier):** Contain only **odd harmonics** (1, 3, 5, 7, 11, 13, …), where triplen harmonics are zero sequence (cancel in line-to-line).

**Line Voltage (R-Y):**
$$v_{RY}(t) = v_{RN}(t) - v_{YN}(t)$$

---

#### Part (b): Single-Phase Cycloconverter

**Principle of Operation:**
A single-phase cycloconverter consists of **two anti-parallel thyristor bridges** (or back-to-back connected thyristors) — called the **Positive (P) bridge** and the **Negative (N) bridge**.

- During the **positive half-cycle** of the desired output, the P-bridge is **active** and modulates the firing angle to produce a positive instantaneous average voltage.
- During the **negative half-cycle** of the output, the N-bridge is active.
- The firing angles of P and N bridges are modulated sinusoidally:
$$\alpha_P(\omega_o t) = \frac{\pi}{2} - \omega_o t$$
$$\alpha_N(\omega_o t) = \frac{3\pi}{2} - \omega_o t$$

**Fundamental Output Voltage Derivation:**

For a **full-bridge (2-pulse) cycloconverter**, the fundamental component of the output voltage is:

$$v_{o1}(t) = \frac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} V_s \sin(\omega_o t)$$

**Derivation sketch:**
The output of a phase-controlled full bridge is:
$$v_o = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\left[\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{3}\cos(3\alpha)\cos(3\omega_i t) + \cdots\right]$$

For P-bridge with $\alpha = \pi/2 - \omega_o t$:
$$v_{o1} = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2} - \omega_o t\right) = \frac{4V_m}{\pi}\sin(\omega_o t)$$

In RMS: $V_{o1,\text{rms}} = \frac{2\sqrt{2} V_s}{\pi} \approx 0.9 V_s$

**Output Frequency Limitation:**
- For **clean output without subharmonics**: $f_{o,\max} = f_i / 3$ (6-pulse bridge).
- Practically acceptable: $f_{o,\max} = f_i / 2$.

**Block Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.7 above.

---

## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING & COMMON PITFALLS

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 
> **1. Mixing Up 180° and 120° Modes:** Many students confuse which devices conduct when. **Remember**: in **180° mode, 3 devices are ON** simultaneously; in **120° mode, only 2 devices are ON**. The **conduction sequence of pairs is identical**, but the device ON-time differs.
> 
> **2. Forgetting Forced Commutation in CSI:** CSI uses thyristors, which **cannot be self-commutated**. Students who draw a CSI without commutation capacitors or load-commutation components will lose 2–3 marks immediately.
> 
> **3. Cycloconverter Frequency Limit:** A common error is stating $f_o$ can be anything. The actual constraint is $f_o < f_i$, and typically $f_o \leq f_i/2$ for a usable waveform.
> 
> **4. SPWM Modulation Index Confusion:** $M = V_r / V_c$ — students often write $M = V_c / V_r$, which is **incorrect by definition**.
> 
> **5. RMS Derivation Sign Errors:** The integral of $\sin^2(\theta)$ over $[\alpha, \pi]$ must include **both** half-cycles (multiply by 2). Forgetting this gives a wrong RMS by factor of $\sqrt{2}$.
> 
> **6. AC-AC Controller Power Factor:** Many students write only the **displacement factor**, forgetting the **distortion factor**. The true PF = (Displacement factor) × (Distortion factor).
> 
> **7. Not Drawing Waveforms:** KTU examiners allocate 2–3 marks specifically for **timing diagrams and waveforms**. Skipping these costs significant marks.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Notes — Keep These on Your Fingertips:**

### A. Current Source Inverter (CSI)
- ✓ DC input is a **stiff current source** (large series inductor L)
- ✓ Output is a **square-wave current** of amplitude $I_d$
- ✓ Uses **thyristors** with **forced or load commutation**
- ✓ Application: **Induction heating, large synchronous motor drives**

### B. 180° vs 120° Conduction Mode
- ✓ **180° mode**: each device ON for 180°, **3 devices** conducting simultaneously
- ✓ **120° mode**: each device ON for 120°, **2 devices** conducting simultaneously
- ✓ Conduction pair sequence is **identical**, only the conduction **duration** differs
- ✓ 180° has higher **voltage utilization**; 120° has higher **reliability**

### C. PWM Inverters
- ✓ **Single-pulse PWM**: 1 pulse per half-cycle, width $2d$ controls output
- ✓ **Multiple-pulse PWM**: N pulses per half-cycle, reduces harmonics
- ✓ **Sinusoidal PWM (SPWM)**: sine reference vs triangle carrier
- ✓ **Modulation index** $M = V_r / V_c$, $0 \leq M \leq 1$ for linear operation
- ✓ **Third-harmonic injection** extends $M_{\max}$ to $2/\sqrt{3}$

### D. AC-AC Voltage Converters
- ✓ Same frequency input/output; only voltage is controlled
- ✓ **Single-phase**: TRIAC or back-to-back SCR pair
- ✓ **Three-phase**: 6 SCRs (fully controlled) or 3 SCRs + 3 diodes (half controlled)
- ✓ **RMS output**: $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\sqrt{1 - \alpha/\pi + \sin(2\alpha)/(2\pi)}$
- ✓ **Power factor** decreases as $\alpha$ increases
- ✓ Modes: ON-OFF (integral cycle) control, Phase-angle control, PWM AC chopper

### E. Cycloconverters
- ✓ **Direct AC-AC frequency converter** — no DC link
- ✓ Uses **two anti-parallel thyristor bridges** (P and N)
- ✓ **Step-down** ($f_o < f_i$): $f_{o,\max} \leq f_i/2$
- ✓ **Output fundamental**: $V_{o1,\text{rms}} = (2\sqrt{2}/\pi) V_s \approx 0.9 V_s$ (single-phase full bridge)
- ✓ Application: **cement mills, rolling mills, ship propulsion, mine hoists**
- ✓ **Forced-commutated step-up cycloconverters** are possible but rarely used

### F. Quick Formula Table

| Formula | Context |
|---|---|
| $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s\sqrt{1 - \alpha/\pi + \sin(2\alpha)/(2\pi)}$ | AC-AC controller (R load) |
| $V_{LL1,\text{rms}} = (\sqrt{6}/\pi) V_s$ | 180° VSI line voltage fundamental |
| $M = V_r / V_c$ | SPWM modulation index |
| $V_{o1} = (2\sqrt{2}/\pi) V_s$ | Cycloconverter fundamental |
| $f_{o,\max} = f_i / 2$ | Cycloconverter frequency limit |
| $V_{o,\text{peak}} = M V_s$ | SPWM peak output |

### G. Key Differences to Remember
- **VSI vs CSI**: V = constant in, I = constant in.
- **Inverter vs Cycloconverter**: Inverter needs DC link; cycloconverter does not.
- **AC-AC Controller vs Cycloconverter**: Controller changes voltage, cycloconverter changes frequency.
- **SPWM vs Multiple-Pulse PWM**: SPWM uses a sine reference; multiple-pulse uses a DC reference with multiple pulses.

### H. Last-Minute Tip
> For numerical problems on AC-AC controllers, **always** check the firing angle range ($\alpha$ between 0 and $\pi$ for R load). For $\alpha = 0$, $V_{o,\text{rms}} = V_s$. For $\alpha = \pi$, $V_{o,\text{rms}} = 0$. **Use these as sanity checks** for your numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DC Motor Control – Phase Control; Single-Phase SCR Drive; Three-Phase SCR Drive; Chopper Controlled DC Drives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT636 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** DC Motor Control – Phase Control; Single-Phase SCR Drive; Three-Phase SCR Drive; Chopper Controlled DC Drives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 DC Motor Speed Control — The Big Picture

A **separately excited DC motor** is one of the most versatile industrial actuators because its speed can be controlled smoothly over a very wide range. The fundamental speed equation is:

$$N = \frac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K \Phi}$$

From this equation, it is evident that the speed $N$ of a DC motor can be controlled by varying any of the following three quantities:

1. **Armature terminal voltage** $V_a$ (Voltage Control / Phase Control / Chopper Control)
2. **Field flux** $\Phi$ (Field Control – covered in earlier modules)
3. **Armature circuit resistance** $R_a$ (Rheostatic Control – inefficient, rarely used)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 4):** This module concentrates on the first method — controlling the **armature voltage** $V_a$ using **power electronic converters**. The converters covered are (a) Phase-Controlled Rectifiers (SCR-based) and (b) DC Choppers (transistor/IGBT-based).

---

### 1.2 Phase Control — Definition

**Phase Control** is the technique of varying the average output voltage of a line-commutated converter (using **Silicon Controlled Rectifiers / Thyristors / SCRs**) by changing the **firing angle** $\alpha$ of the thyristors. The thyristor is triggered at a controllable instant after the natural commutation point of the supply voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Term — Firing Angle (α):** The angle (measured in electrical degrees or radians) measured from the instant the thyristor *would have* turned on naturally (i.e., the instant a diode would conduct) to the instant it is *actually* gated. Range: $0° \le \alpha \le 180°$ for full converter operation with resistive load, and practically up to $90°$ for DC motor loads.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine the AC mains as a **pressurised water pipe**, and the SCR as a **smart tap (valve)** that you can open only for a *part* of every half-cycle. The later you open the tap (larger $\alpha$), the **less water flows per second**, and thus the **lower the average pressure at the motor terminal**.

- **Tap fully open at the start of every half-cycle** $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha = 0°$ $\Rightarrow$ Maximum DC voltage $\Rightarrow$ Maximum motor speed.
- **Tap opened very late in the half-cycle** $\Rightarrow$ $\alpha \to 90°$ (or higher) $\Rightarrow$ Low DC voltage $\Rightarrow$ Low motor speed.

This "delayed opening" of the valve, repeatedly every half-cycle, is precisely what **phase control** does with thyristors.

### 1.4 SCR Drive vs Chopper Drive — What is the Difference?

> [!NOTE]
> **SCR Drive:** Uses a **rectifier** that converts fixed-frequency AC mains into a *variable-average* DC voltage by phase control. Suitable for **high-power, moderate-bandwidth** applications (e.g., large DC motors in rolling mills, paper machines, elevators, mine hoists).

> [!NOTE]
> **Chopper Drive:** Uses a **DC–DC switching converter** that chops a *fixed* DC source (battery or rectified mains) into a variable-duty-cycle, high-frequency PWM waveform. The average voltage is varied by changing the **duty cycle D**. Suitable for **battery-powered vehicles, servo drives, traction**.

| Feature | SCR Drive (Phase Control) | Chopper Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Input supply | AC mains | Fixed DC |
| Switching device | Thyristor (SCR) | Transistor / IGBT / MOSFET |
| Control variable | Firing angle $\alpha$ | Duty cycle $D$ |
| Switching frequency | Low (synchronous with mains) | High (kHz range) |
| Commutation | Line (natural) commutation | Forced commutation needed |
| Typical power level | High (kW to MW) | Low to medium (W to tens of kW) |
| Applications | Steel mills, elevators | EV traction, robotics |

---

### 1.5 Four-Quadrant Operation — A Key KTU Concept

A DC motor drive is often classified by the number of quadrants in which it can operate. The four quadrants of the speed–torque plane are:

- **Quadrant I**: Forward motoring (positive speed, positive torque)
- **Quadrant II**: Forward braking / regeneration
- **Quadrant III**: Reverse motoring
- **Quadrant IV**: Reverse braking / regeneration

A **fully-controlled bridge converter** with a **freewheeling action** allows motoring in one direction and plugging/braking in the opposite direction. A **dual converter** (two fully-controlled bridges connected in anti-parallel) provides full **four-quadrant** operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Whenever a numerical problem on DC drives is given, the examiner expects you to explicitly state the *operating mode* (motoring, braking, plugging) and the *quadrant* along with the average voltage equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Phase-Controlled SCR Drive

The basic building block is the **single-phase fully-controlled bridge** (also called a *full converter* or *Graetz bridge* with four SCRs). The firing angle $\alpha$ of the SCRs is varied to control the average DC output voltage applied to the motor armature.

**Step-by-step operating logic:**

1. The AC supply voltage $v_s = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ is applied to the bridge.
2. Each SCR pair ($T_1$–$T_2$ or $T_3$–$T_4$) conducts for $180°$ once gated.
3. Conduction is transferred from one pair to the other at the instant of commutation, which occurs **$\alpha$** after the natural commutation point.
4. The output voltage waveform $v_o$ consists of portions of the sinusoidal supply, displaced by angle $\alpha$.
5. The average value $V_o$ of this waveform depends on $\alpha$ as $V_o = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$.
6. As $\alpha$ increases, $\cos\alpha$ decreases, and the average voltage falls — hence motor speed reduces.

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous vs Discontinuous Conduction:** For a DC motor load (which has inductance $L_a$), conduction is typically *continuous* provided the armature current never falls to zero. For light loads, conduction may become *discontinuous* and the average voltage relation changes.

---

### 2.2 Single-Phase SCR Drive — Circuit Variants

| Circuit | Configuration | $V_o$ Expression (Continuous Conduction) |
|---|---|---|
| **Half-Wave** (1 SCR, 1 FD) | Single-Quadrant | $V_o = \dfrac{V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ |
| **Semi-Converter** (2 SCR + 2 FD) | Single-Quadrant, Freewheeling | $V_o = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ |
| **Full Converter** (4 SCR) | Two-Quadrant, no freewheel | $V_o = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ |
| **Dual Converter** (2 × Full Converter) | Four-Quadrant | $V_o = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ (per bridge) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freewheeling Diode (FWD):** A diode connected across the armature that conducts whenever the armature current tends to become negative, allowing reactive energy stored in $L_a$ to freewheel through the diode and the armature. This prevents the negative portion of the output voltage from being applied to the motor and improves the input power factor.

---

### 2.3 Three-Phase SCR Drive — Circuit Variants

| Circuit | Configuration | $V_o$ Expression (Continuous Conduction) |
|---|---|---|
| **3-φ Half-Wave** (3 SCR) | Single-Quadrant | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha = \dfrac{3V_{LL,m}}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$ |
| **3-φ Semi-Converter** (3 SCR + 3 FD) | Single-Quadrant, Freewheeling | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ |
| **3-φ Full Converter** (6 SCR) | Two-Quadrant | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ |
| **3-φ Dual Converter** (2 × Full) | Four-Quadrant | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3} V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three-phase is preferred at high power:** Three-phase converters draw balanced currents from the supply, produce less ripple in the output (lowest ripple frequency = $6f_s$), have a higher **form factor** efficiency, and have better utilisation of the supply transformer.

---

### 2.4 Chopper Controlled DC Drives

A **chopper** is a static device that converts a fixed DC input to a variable DC output by **chopping** the input voltage at a high switching frequency. The average output voltage is controlled by varying the **duty cycle** $D = T_{on}/T$.

**Basic Step-Down (Buck) Chopper driving a DC Motor:**

When the chopper switch is ON for time $T_{on}$, the motor is connected to source $V_s$; when OFF, the motor freewheels through a diode.

$$V_o = D \cdot V_s = \frac{T_{on}}{T} V_s$$

The motor's average armature voltage becomes $V_a = D \cdot V_s$, and hence:

$$N = \frac{D V_s - I_a R_a}{K\Phi}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classes of Chopper Operation for DC Motor Drive:**
> - **Class A (First-Quadrant Chopper):** Motoring in one direction only. Switch + diode in series across the motor.
> - **Class B (Second-Quadrant Chopper):** Regenerative braking. Energy returned to the source.
> - **Class C (Two-Quadrant Chopper):** Class A + Class B; motoring + regen braking in one direction.
> - **Class D (Four-Quadrant Chopper / H-Bridge):** Forward + reverse motoring + braking.

---

### 2.5 ⚡ KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (HIGH-YIELD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU University Exam question from this module.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applicable Circuit / Mode |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Average output voltage, full converter, 1-φ | $V_o = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | Single-phase full bridge, continuous conduction |
| 2 | RMS output voltage, 1-φ full converter | $V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \dfrac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}$ | 1-φ full converter |
| 3 | Average output, 1-φ semi-converter | $V_o = \dfrac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | 1-φ semi-converter |
| 4 | Average output, 3-φ full converter | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ | 3-φ full converter |
| 5 | Average output, 3-φ semi-converter | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$ | 3-φ semi-converter |
| 6 | Average output, 3-φ half-wave | $V_o = \dfrac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$ | 3-φ half converter |
| 7 | Average voltage, step-down chopper | $V_o = D \cdot V_s$ | Class A chopper |
| 8 | RMS voltage, step-down chopper | $V_{rms} = V_s\sqrt{D}$ | Class A chopper |
| 9 | DC motor back EMF | $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a$ | All DC drives |
| 10 | DC motor speed | $N = \dfrac{V_a - I_a R_a}{K\Phi}$ | All DC drives |
| 11 | DC motor torque | $T = K\Phi I_a$ | All DC drives |
| 12 | Power delivered to armature | $P_a = V_a I_a = E_b I_a + I_a^2 R_a$ | All DC drives |
| 13 | Power converted (developed) | $P_m = E_b I_a = T \omega$ | All DC drives |
| 14 | Firing angle for given speed | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi V_a}{2V_m}\right)$ | 1-φ full converter |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $V_m$ = peak phase voltage of AC supply
> - $V_{LL,m}$ = peak line-to-line voltage
> - $V_s$ = fixed DC source voltage (chopper)
> - $D = T_{on}/T$ = chopper duty cycle
> - $\alpha$ = firing angle (electrical degrees or radians — be consistent!)
> - $K$ = machine constant, $\Phi$ = field flux (Wb)

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Steel Rolling Mills** — Large DC motors fed by 3-φ fully-controlled converters for precise torque and speed control.
- **Paper Machines** — Multi-motor coordinated drives using SCR converters for sectional speed matching.
- **Mine Winders and Hoists** — Four-quadrant dual-converter drives for controlled acceleration, motoring, and regenerative braking on downhills.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs)** — Chopper-fed (now MOSFET/IGBT-based) DC motor drives for battery-operated traction.
- **Battery-Powered Robotics and Forklifts** — Class C/D chopper drives for efficient forward/reverse motoring and braking.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of 1-φ Full Converter (Continuous Conduction)

The single-phase fully-controlled bridge feeds a highly inductive DC motor load. Conduction is continuous and each thyristor pair conducts for $180°$. The output voltage waveform consists of sinusoidal segments shifted by the firing angle $\alpha$.

The output voltage during conduction of the positive pair is:
$$v_o(\omega t) = V_m \sin(\omega t), \quad \text{for } \alpha \le \omega t \le \alpha + \pi$$

The average value of the output voltage over one full cycle ($0$ to $2\pi$) is:

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

Step-by-step evaluation:

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi}
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\alpha + \pi) + \cos(\alpha) \Big]
$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\cos(\alpha + \pi) = -\cos\alpha$:

$$
V_o = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -(-\cos\alpha) + \cos\alpha \Big] = \frac{V_m}{2\pi} (2\cos\alpha)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \cos\alpha\,}
$$

This is the **most important formula** for KTU 1-φ SCR drive problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: RMS Output Voltage of 1-φ Full Converter

The RMS value is needed for calculating the form factor, ripple, and heating of the motor armature.

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, d(\omega t)
$$

Using $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi} \big[1 - \cos(2\omega t)\big] d(\omega t)
$$

Integrating:

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \Big[ (\omega t) - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2} \Big]_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi}
$$

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \Big[ \pi - \frac{\sin(2\alpha + 2\pi) - \sin(2\alpha)}{2} \Big]
$$

Since $\sin(2\alpha + 2\pi) = \sin(2\alpha)$, the second term vanishes:

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{4\pi} \cdot \pi = \frac{V_m^2}{4} \quad \text{(for }\alpha=0\text{)}
$$

Generalising for any $\alpha$:

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{rms} = V_m \sqrt{\frac{1}{\pi}\left(\pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right)}\,}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of 3-φ Full Converter

In a 3-φ fully-controlled bridge feeding a DC motor (continuous conduction), the output waveform consists of six line-to-line voltage segments per cycle. Each segment has a duration of $60°$, and conduction is shifted by $\alpha$ from the natural commutation point.

Taking one such segment — say $V_{ab}$ conducting from $\alpha$ to $\alpha + 60°$:

$$
v_o(\omega t) = \sqrt{3}\,V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°), \quad \alpha \le \omega t \le \alpha + \frac{\pi}{3}
$$

The average over a full $2\pi$ cycle (six such segments):

$$
V_o = \frac{3}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3} \sqrt{3}\,V_m \sin(\omega t + 30°)\, d(\omega t)
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t + 30°) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\alpha + \pi/3}
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\alpha + \pi/3 + \pi/6) + \cos(\alpha + \pi/6) \Big]
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\alpha + \pi/2) + \cos(\alpha + \pi/6) \Big]
$$

Using $\cos(\alpha + \pi/2) = -\sin\alpha$:

$$
V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \Big[ \sin\alpha + \cos(\alpha + \pi/6) \Big]
$$

Expanding $\cos(\alpha + \pi/6) = \cos\alpha \cos 30° - \sin\alpha \sin 30° = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha$:

$$
V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \left[ \sin\alpha + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha - \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha \right]
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \left[ \frac{1}{2}\sin\alpha + \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\cos\alpha \right]
$$

Recognising this as $\sin(\alpha + 60°)$:

$$
V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \sin(\alpha + 60°)
$$

**Reformulating using cosine (KTU standard form):** Since $\sin(\alpha + 60°) = \cos(\alpha - 30°) = \cos(30° - \alpha)$, and KTU textbooks conventionally use the cosine form (with $\alpha$ measured from the natural commutation point of the *line* voltage):

$$
\boxed{\,V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** In the **3-φ half-wave** converter, the same constant $\frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$ appears (i.e., *half* of the full converter). The $\cos\alpha$ form uses $\alpha$ measured from the natural commutation of the *line-to-line* voltage in a full bridge.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Average Output Voltage of a Step-Down Chopper

A step-down chopper feeding a DC motor (with freewheeling diode across the motor terminals) is shown schematically in Section 4. The chopper switch is ON for $T_{on}$ and OFF for $T_{off}$, with period $T = T_{on} + T_{off}$.

During the **ON** state, the motor sees the source voltage $V_s$.
During the **OFF** state, the freewheeling diode conducts and the motor sees zero volts (assuming ideal diode and negligible ripple in armature current).

The average voltage over one period is:

$$
V_o = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s\, dt + \frac{1}{T} \int_{T_{on}}^{T} 0\, dt
$$

$$
V_o = \frac{V_s \cdot T_{on}}{T}
$$

Defining the **duty cycle** $D = T_{on}/T$:

$$
\boxed{\,V_o = D \cdot V_s\,}
$$

The RMS value (important for computing armature copper loss and heating):

$$
V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^{T_{on}} V_s^2\, dt = V_s^2 D
$$

$$
\boxed{\,V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{D}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-Up Chopper (Boost) for Regenerative Braking:** During braking, the motor acts as a generator, and a step-up chopper can return the kinetic energy to the source. The relevant relation is $V_s = \frac{V_o}{1 - D}$, where now $V_o$ is the motor-side voltage and $V_s$ is the source-side (higher) voltage.

---

### 3.5 Combined Analytical Matrix — DC Motor with SCR/Chopper Drive

The **general procedure** for solving KTU numerical problems on DC drives is:

| Step | Task | Equation Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Identify converter type and find average $V_a$ | From converter formula table |
| 2 | Find firing angle $\alpha$ from given $V_a$ (or vice versa) | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}(\pi V_a / 2V_m)$ for 1-φ full converter |
| 3 | Compute back EMF $E_b$ | $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a$ |
| 4 | Compute speed $N$ (if $\Phi$ given) | $N = E_b / (K\Phi)$ |
| 5 | Compute developed torque $T$ | $T = K\Phi I_a$ |
| 6 | Compute developed power $P_m$ | $P_m = T \omega = E_b I_a$ |
| 7 | Check operating mode | If $V_a > E_b$: motoring; if $V_a < E_b$: braking |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to **convert peak values to RMS** (or vice versa). Always state whether the supply voltage given is RMS ($V_s$) or peak ($V_m$). If RMS, use $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_s$. If line-to-line RMS is given for a 3-φ circuit, $V_{LL,m} = \sqrt{2}\,V_{LL,rms}$ and $V_{ph,m} = \sqrt{2}\,V_{ph,rms}$.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: 1-φ Full Converter DC Drive

> **Problem:** A 220 V, 50 Hz AC supply is applied to a single-phase full converter feeding a separately excited DC motor with armature resistance $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, armature inductance $L_a = 10$ mH, and field constant $K\Phi = 0.5$ V·s/rad. The motor runs at 1500 rpm drawing an average current of 30 A. Find (i) the firing angle, (ii) the back EMF, (iii) the developed torque, and (iv) the power developed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the circuit and the average voltage:**

For a 1-φ full converter with continuous conduction:
$V_a = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$

Given $V_s = 220$ V (RMS, assumed), $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 220 = 311.13$ V.

The motor speed equation gives us $E_b$:
$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = K\Phi \cdot \omega$

Angular speed: $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 157.08$ rad/s

$E_b = 0.5 \times 157.08 = 78.54$ V

**Step 2 — Back-calculate the required $V_a$:**

$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a = 78.54 + 30 \times 0.5 = 78.54 + 15 = 93.54$ V

**Step 3 — Firing angle:**

$$
\cos\alpha = \frac{\pi V_a}{2 V_m} = \frac{\pi \times 93.54}{2 \times 311.13} = \frac{293.91}{622.26} = 0.4724
$$

$$
\boxed{\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.4724) \approx 61.81°}
$$

**Step 4 — Developed torque:**

$$
T = K\Phi \cdot I_a = 0.5 \times 30 = 15\ \text{N·m}
$$

**Step 5 — Developed power:**

$$
P_m = T \cdot \omega = 15 \times 157.08 = 2356.2\ \text{W} \approx 2.36\ \text{kW}
$$

**Verification:** $P_m = E_b \cdot I_a = 78.54 \times 30 = 2356.2$ W ✓

---

### 3.7 Worked Example: Chopper-Fed DC Drive

> **Problem:** A 200 V DC source feeds a step-down chopper connected to a DC motor with $R_a = 0.4\ \Omega$ and $K\Phi = 0.8$ V·s/rad. The motor draws 25 A at a duty cycle of 0.6. Find (i) the average armature voltage, (ii) the back EMF, and (iii) the speed.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Average armature voltage:**
$V_a = D \cdot V_s = 0.6 \times 200 = 120$ V

**Step 2 — Back EMF:**
$E_b = V_a - I_a R_a = 120 - 25 \times 0.4 = 120 - 10 = 110$ V

**Step 3 — Speed:**
$\omega = \dfrac{E_b}{K\Phi} = \dfrac{110}{0.8} = 137.5$ rad/s

$$
N = \frac{60 \omega}{2\pi} = \frac{60 \times 137.5}{2\pi} \approx 1313.2\ \text{rpm}
$$

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Single-Phase Full Converter (Graetz Bridge) Driving a DC Motor

The complete power circuit showing the four SCRs ($T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$), the AC source, the DC motor armature ($R_a, L_a, E_b$), and the freewheeling diode (FWD) across the armature:

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[AC Supply vs Vm sin wt] --> T1
    AC --> T3
    T1[SCR T1] --> DCOUT
    T2[SCR T2] --> DCOUT
    T3[SCR T3] --> DCOUT
    T4[SCR T4] --> DCOUT
    DCOUT[DC Output Node] --> LA
    LA[Armature La] --> RA
    RA[Armature Ra] --> EB
    EB[Back EMF Eb] --> FWD
    FWD[Freewheel Diode] --> DCOUT
    GATE1[Gate Pulse alpha to T1 T2] -.-> T1
    GATE1 -.-> T2
    GATE2[Gate Pulse pi plus alpha to T3 T4] -.-> T3
    GATE2 -.-> T4
```

**Operating mode:** Positive group ($T_1, T_2$) gated at $\omega t = \alpha$; negative group ($T_3, T_4$) gated at $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$. Output average $V_o = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$.

---

### 4.2 Three-Phase Full Converter Driving a DC Motor

```mermaid
graph TB
    S3P[3 Phase AC Source Van Vbn Vcn] --> BR
    BR[6 SCR Full Bridge T1 T2 T3 T4 T5 T6] --> PNODE
    PNODE[Positive DC Bus] --> FWD
    FWD[Freewheel Diode] --> NNNODE
    NNNODE[Negative DC Bus] --> BR
    PNODE --> ARM
    ARM[DC Motor Armature La Ra Eb] --> NNNODE
    GP[Sequential Gate Pulses every 60 deg] -.-> BR
```

**Operating mode:** Six SCRs fired in sequence every $60°$. Each SCR conducts for $120°$. Output average $V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ (continuous conduction).

---

### 4.3 Step-Down Chopper Feeding a DC Motor (Class A Operation)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VS[DC Source Vs] --> SW
    SW[Chopper Switch IGBT or BJT] --> MOTOR
    MOTOR[DC Motor Armature La Ra Eb] --> RTN
    RTN[Return] --> VS
    FWD2[Freewheel Diode] --> SW
    FWD2 --> MOTOR
    CTRL[PWM Controller Ton T period] -.-> SW
```

**Operating logic:**
- **Switch ON ($0 \le t \le T_{on}$):** Motor connected to $V_s$. Current rises through $L_a$.
- **Switch OFF ($T_{on} \le t \le T$):** FWD conducts, motor current freewheels. Voltage across motor ≈ 0.
- **Average motor voltage:** $V_a = D \cdot V_s$ where $D = T_{on}/T$.

---

### 4.4 Two-Quadrant Chopper (Class C — Forward Motoring + Regen Braking)

```mermaid
graph TB
    VSS[DC Source Vs] --> S1
    S1[Switch S1 IGBT] --> MOTO
    S1 --> D1
    D1[Diode D1] --> VSS
    MOTO[DC Motor] --> S2
    S2[Switch S2 IGBT] --> RTN2
    S2 --> D2
    D2[Diode D2] --> MOTO
    RTN2[Return to Source]
    CT2[Controller Selects Mode] -.-> S1
    CT2 -.-> S2
```

**Modes:**
- **Motoring (Quadrant I):** S1 modulated, S2 OFF. $V_a = D V_s$ (positive).
- **Regenerative Braking (Quadrant II):** S2 modulated, S1 OFF. Motor acts as generator, $D_2$ returns energy to source.

---

### 4.5 H-Bridge Four-Quadrant Chopper (Class D)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VSP[DC Source Positive] --> SA
    SA[S1] --> MA
    MA[Motor Terminal A] --> MB
    MB[Motor Terminal B] --> SD
    SD[S4] --> VSN
    VSN[DC Source Negative]
    SA --> D3
    D3 --> VSN
    SD --> D2
    D2 --> VSP
    VSP --> SC
    SC[S3] --> MB
    MB --> D4
    D4 --> VSN
    VSN --> SB
    SB[S2] --> MA
    MA --> D1
    D1 --> VSP
    CT3[H Bridge PWM Controller] -.-> SA
    CT3 -.-> SB
    CT3 -.-> SC
    CT3 -.-> SD
```

**Modes:**
- **Q1 (Forward Motoring):** S1 & S4 ON
- **Q2 (Forward Braking):** S2 & S3 ON (regen through D1, D4)
- **Q3 (Reverse Motoring):** S2 & S3 ON
- **Q4 (Reverse Braking):** S1 & S4 ON (regen through D2, D3)

---

### 4.6 Block Diagram of a Complete Closed-Loop DC Drive

```mermaid
graph TB
    REF[Speed Reference Nref] --> SUM
    SUM[Summing Junction] --> CTRL
    CTRL[PI Speed Controller] --> FIRCALC
    FIRCALC[Firing Angle Calculator] --> PULSE
    PULSE[Gate Pulse Generator] --> CONV
    CONV[Converter SCR or Chopper] --> MOT
    MOT[DC Motor] --> LOAD
    LOAD[Mechanical Load]
    MOT --> TACH
    TACH[Tachogenerator Speed Feedback] --> SUM
```

**Closed-loop control logic:** The actual speed is sensed by a tachogenerator and compared to the reference. The error is processed by a PI controller, which adjusts the firing angle $\alpha$ (or duty cycle $D$) to maintain the desired speed under varying load conditions.
