## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q1. What is meant by phase control in an SCR-based DC drive? How does it achieve speed control of a DC motor?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Phase control is the technique of varying the **average output voltage** of a thyristor (SCR) rectifier by changing the instant at which the thyristor is triggered within each half-cycle of the AC supply. The delay between the natural commutation point and the actual gate pulse is called the **firing angle $\alpha$**.

For a single-phase full converter, the average output voltage is $V_o = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$. By increasing $\alpha$ from $0°$ to $90°$, $V_o$ decreases from a maximum positive value to zero. Since the speed of a separately excited DC motor is $N = (V_a - I_a R_a) / K\Phi$, reducing $V_a$ reduces the speed. Thus, by varying $\alpha$, the motor speed is smoothly controlled.

*(Expected keywords for full marks: firing angle, average voltage, $V_o = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$, speed equation.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Q2. Distinguish between a chopper-controlled DC drive and an SCR-controlled DC drive. List two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | SCR-Controlled Drive | Chopper-Controlled Drive |
|---|---|---|
| Supply | AC mains | Fixed DC source (e.g., battery) |
| Control variable | Firing angle $\alpha$ | Duty cycle $D$ |
| Switching frequency | Mains frequency (50/60 Hz) | High (1–20 kHz) |
| Commutation | Line (natural) commutation | Forced commutation required |

**Applications:**
- *SCR drives:* Steel rolling mills, mine winders, paper machines, large elevators.
- *Chopper drives:* Battery-powered electric vehicles, robotics, forklift traction, servo drives.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

#### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **single-phase fully-controlled bridge converter** feeding a separately excited DC motor. Derive the expression for the average output voltage in the continuous conduction mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-phase full converter is used to control the speed of a 220 V, 1500 rpm, 10 A separately excited DC motor. The armature resistance is $R_a = 1\ \Omega$ and the field constant is $K\Phi = 0.5$ V·s/rad. The supply is 230 V, 50 Hz. Find:
- (i) The firing angle required to run the motor at 1000 rpm at rated torque.
- (ii) The motor torque at $\alpha = 60°$ and speed 1200 rpm.
- (iii) The power factor of the supply at $\alpha = 60°$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Circuit description (3 marks):**

The single-phase fully-controlled bridge consists of **four thyristors** $T_1, T_2, T_3, T_4$ arranged as two legs across the AC supply. The DC motor armature ($R_a, L_a$, back EMF $E_b$) is connected across the DC output terminals. A **freewheeling diode (FWD)** is connected across the armature to provide a path for the inductive armature current when the converter output voltage goes negative.

**Operation (2 marks):**

During the **positive half-cycle** of the supply, when thyristors $T_1$ and $T_2$ are gated at $\omega t = \alpha$, they conduct and the load is connected to the supply through them. The output voltage follows the supply, $v_o = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, from $\omega t = \alpha$ to $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$. At $\omega t = \pi + \alpha$, $T_3$ and $T_4$ are gated and they take over conduction (commutation occurs naturally as $T_1$ and $T_2$ become reverse-biased). The output voltage then becomes $-V_m\sin(\omega t)$ (effectively the negative of the previous waveform, since line-commutated). Each thyristor conducts for $180°$.

**Derivation (2 marks):**

As derived in Section 3.1:
$$
V_o = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\alpha+\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, d(\omega t) = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:**
- Motor: 220 V, 1500 rpm, 10 A rated
- $R_a = 1\ \Omega$, $K\Phi = 0.5$ V·s/rad
- Supply: 230 V, 50 Hz
- $V_m = \sqrt{2} \times 230 = 325.27$ V

**(i) Firing angle at 1000 rpm, rated torque:** (3 marks)

Rated torque: $T_{rated} = K\Phi \times I_a = 0.5 \times 10 = 5$ N·m.
For "rated torque," the armature current is 10 A (since $T \propto I_a$ with constant field).

$\omega_{1000} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1000}{60} = 104.72$ rad/s

$E_b = K\Phi \cdot \omega = 0.5 \times 104.72 = 52.36$ V

$V_a = E_b + I_a R_a = 52.36 + 10 \times 1 = 62.36$ V

$\cos\alpha = \dfrac{\pi V_a}{2V_m} = \dfrac{\pi \times 62.36}{2 \times 325.27} = \dfrac{195.92}{650.54} = 0.3012$

$\alpha = \cos^{-1}(0.3012) \approx \mathbf{72.46°}$

**(ii) Torque at $\alpha = 60°$ and 1200 rpm:** (2 marks)

$V_a = \dfrac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos 60° = \dfrac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \times 0.5 = 103.53$ V

$\omega_{1200} = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 125.66$ rad/s

$E_b = K\Phi \cdot \omega = 0.5 \times 125.66 = 62.83$ V

$I_a = \dfrac{V_a - E_b}{R_a} = \dfrac{103.53 - 62.83}{1} = 40.7$ A

$T = K\Phi \cdot I_a = 0.5 \times 40.7 = \mathbf{20.35\ N\cdot m}$

**(iii) Power factor at $\alpha = 60°$:** (2 marks)

For a 1-φ full converter with highly inductive load, the supply current is a quasi-square wave of amplitude $I_a$. The displacement factor is $\cos\alpha$. The distortion factor is $\dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi}$ (the ratio of fundamental RMS to total RMS for a square wave). Therefore:

$\text{Power factor} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cos\alpha = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \times 0.5 = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \approx \mathbf{0.45\ (lagging)}$

---

#### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain with a neat circuit diagram the operation of a **three-phase fully-controlled bridge converter** feeding a DC motor. Derive the average output voltage expression. Compare its performance with a single-phase full converter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **step-down chopper** supplies a DC motor from a 220 V battery. The motor has $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$ and $L_a = 20$ mH. The chopping frequency is 1 kHz with a duty cycle of 0.4. Assuming continuous conduction:
- (i) Calculate the average armature voltage and current.
- (ii) Calculate the RMS armature current ripple.
- (iii) If the motor back EMF is 80 V, find the speed and developed torque. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

**Circuit description (2 marks):** Six thyristors $T_1 \ldots T_6$ arranged as three legs. AC source is 3-φ, output is taken across the DC bus. Each thyristor conducts for $120°$ and is fired in sequence every $60°$.

**Operation (2 marks):** During any $60°$ interval, one thyristor from the positive group ($T_1, T_3, T_5$) and one from the negative group ($T_4, T_6, T_2$) conduct, applying a line-to-line voltage to the load. The output is a six-step waveform with lowest ripple frequency = $6f_s = 300$ Hz.

**Derivation (2 marks):** As derived in Section 3.3:
$$
V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

**Comparison with 1-φ (1 mark):**

| Aspect | 1-φ Full Converter | 3-φ Full Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Ripple frequency | $2f_s = 100$ Hz | $6f_s = 300$ Hz |
| Output ripple amplitude | Higher | Lower (smoother) |
| Maximum average voltage (at $\alpha=0$) | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637\,V_m$ | $\frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi} \approx 1.654\,V_m$ |
| Power level | Low to medium | High |
| Transformer utilisation | Poor | Good |
| Supply current balance | Unbalanced | Balanced |

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

**Given:** $V_s = 220$ V, $f = 1000$ Hz, $D = 0.4$, $R_a = 0.5\ \Omega$, $L_a = 20$ mH, $E_b = 80$ V.

**Period:** $T = 1/f = 1$ ms. $T_{on} = D \cdot T = 0.4$ ms. $T_{off} = 0.6$ ms.

**(i) Average armature voltage and current:** (2 marks)

$V_a = D \cdot V_s = 0.4 \times 220 = \mathbf{88\ V}$

$I_a = \dfrac{V_a - E_b}{R_a} = \dfrac{88 - 80}{0.5} = \mathbf{16\ A}$

**(ii) RMS current ripple:** (3 marks)

The peak-to-peak ripple in armature current for a step-down chopper (with continuous conduction) is:

$\Delta I = \dfrac{(V_s - E_b) \cdot T_{on}}{L_a} = \dfrac{(220 - 80) \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{140 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{0.056}{0.020} = 2.8$ A

(The negative excursion during $T_{off}$ has magnitude $\dfrac{E_b \cdot T_{off}}{L_a} = \dfrac{80 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-3}}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.4$ A — confirms the ripple is bounded by these two values.)

For a triangular ripple about the average, the **RMS ripple current** is:
$I_{ripple,rms} = \dfrac{\Delta I}{2\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{2.8}{2\sqrt{3}} \approx \mathbf{0.808\ A}$

(Use whichever bound is larger; the more conservative answer is to use $\Delta I = 2.8$ A.)

**(iii) Speed and developed torque:** (2 marks)

Need $K\Phi$ from the rated data. From the steady state at $V_a = 88$ V and $I_a = 16$ A:
$E_b = 80$ V $\Rightarrow$ $K\Phi \cdot \omega = 80$

But we need another data point. The problem provides $E_b = 80$ V directly, so we compute $K\Phi$ by assuming a rated point. Without rated $N$, the conventional assumption is to take the developed torque:
$T = K\Phi \cdot I_a$

Without explicit $K\Phi$, express the **speed** directly from $E_b$:
$\omega = E_b / (K\Phi) = 80 / (K\Phi)$

In KTU problems, $K\Phi$ is usually given. Assuming $K\Phi = 0.5$ V·s/rad (a common textbook value):
$\omega = 80 / 0.5 = 160$ rad/s
$N = \dfrac{60 \times 160}{2\pi} \approx 1528.9$ rpm

$T = 0.5 \times 16 = 8$ N·m

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Mixing up RMS and Peak:** A surprisingly large number of students substitute $V_s = 220$ V directly into $V_o = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ without computing $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_s$. **Always state which value is RMS and which is peak.**
> 2. **Forgetting $I_a R_a$ drop:** Beginners often write $E_b = V_a$ without the armature drop. At low speeds, $I_a R_a$ is comparable to $E_b$ and *cannot* be ignored.
> 3. **Wrong $\alpha$ limits:** For a DC motor (inductive load), practical $\alpha$ is between $0°$ and $90°$. Going beyond $90°$ puts the converter in *inverter* mode, which can damage the system if not designed for it.
> 4. **Confusing chopper and SCR drive formulas:** $V_a = D V_s$ is for **chopper**; $V_a = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$ is for **1-φ full SCR converter**. Mixing them is a guaranteed 7-mark loss.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the operating quadrant:** KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the student does not clearly state whether the drive is in motoring, braking, or plugging mode.
> 6. **Sign convention for dual converter:** In a dual converter, the bridge with firing angle $\alpha$ supplies positive $V_o$ and the bridge with $\pi - \alpha$ supplies negative $V_o$. Do NOT write the negative bridge as $\cos(\pi - \alpha)$ directly without explanation.
> 7. **Units inconsistency in numericals:** Use SI throughout — speeds in rad/s for $\omega$, EMF in volts, current in amperes, torque in N·m. Mixing rpm, N, and V will lead to errors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: DC Motor Control**

- ⚡ DC motor speed is controlled primarily by **armature voltage variation**; the relation is $N = (V_a - I_a R_a)/(K\Phi)$.
- ⚡ **Phase control** varies the firing angle $\alpha$ of SCRs to control the average output voltage of a rectifier.
- ⚡ For a **1-φ full converter**: $V_o = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$, valid for **continuous conduction**.
- ⚡ For a **1-φ semi-converter** (with FWD): $V_o = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha)$.
- ⚡ For a **3-φ full converter**: $V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha$.
- ⚡ For a **3-φ half-wave**: $V_o = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$.
- ⚡ A **3-φ full converter** has 6-pulse output (ripple frequency = $6f_s$) — much smoother than 1-φ.
- ⚡ A **dual converter** (two bridges in anti-parallel) provides **four-quadrant** operation.
- ⚡ A **chopper** is a DC–DC converter; **step-down chopper** gives $V_o = D \cdot V_s$.
- ⚡ **Duty cycle** $D = T_{on}/T$ controls the average output; **higher $D \Rightarrow$ higher speed**.
- ⚡ Chopper classes: **A** (1-quadrant), **B** (regen only), **C** (2-quadrant), **D** (H-bridge, 4-quadrant).
- ⚡ The **firing angle** $\alpha$ and **duty cycle** $D$ are the two fundamental control knobs in power-electronic DC drives.
- ⚡ When solving numericals: convert supply voltage to peak $V_m = \sqrt{2} V_s$, compute $V_a$, find $E_b = V_a - I_a R_a$, then speed/torque/power.
- ⚡ The **ripple** in output current is inversely proportional to chopping frequency and load inductance; high $f$ and large $L_a$ ⇒ smoother current.
- ⚡ For **regenerative braking** with chopper, the chopper operates in **step-up (boost)** mode to pump energy back to the source.
- ⚡ KTU exam favourite question types: (a) derive average $V_o$ for 1-φ / 3-φ full converter, (b) numerical on firing angle for a given speed, (c) chopper numerical with ripple current, (d) circuit + waveform explanation with quadrant identification.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: AC Motor Control: Controlled Slip System and Slip Power Recovery System; Stepper Motor Drive
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT636 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** AC Motor Control: Controlled Slip System and Slip Power Recovery System; Stepper Motor Drive

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to AC Motor Speed Control (Slip-Ring Induction Motor)

The **induction motor** is a workhorse of industry, but its Achilles' heel has always been efficient speed control. Unlike DC motors, the induction motor's speed is fundamentally tied to the supply frequency and the motor's slip. The most efficient methods to control the speed of a **Slip-Ring (Wound-Rotor) Induction Motor** revolve around manipulating the rotor circuit, which is externally accessible. Module 4 focuses on two main power-electronic strategies: the **Controlled Slip System (Stator Voltage Control applied with rotor resistance)** and the **Slip Power Recovery System (SPRS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:**
> This module is critical because it bridges the gap between **passive rotor control** (resistance) and **active power electronic rotor control** (rectifier-inverter). Examiners frequently ask comparison questions between these two methods. **Pay special attention to power flow direction and efficiency metrics.**

## 1.2 Controlled Slip System — Intuitive Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
The *Controlled Slip System* refers to the method of varying the **stator voltage** (using a pair of anti-parallel thyristors in each phase — an AC-AC phase-controlled regulator) applied to the stator of an induction motor, while the rotor may either be short-circuited or connected to external star-connected resistances. The torque developed is proportional to the square of the stator voltage, allowing a controlled reduction in speed.

**Intuitive Analogy: "The Water Tap & the Sloping Pipe"** 🚰

Imagine a tap (stator voltage) controlling the flow of water into a horizontal pipe (induction motor). The pipe has a small adjustable valve at its end (rotor resistance).
- If you **partially close the tap** (reduce voltage), the water rushes in with less force — the motor produces less torque, and under load, the **"slip"** increases (the rotor "drags" behind the synchronous speed more).
- This method is simple, cheap, and bi-directional (using back-to-back thyristors, you can run the motor in both directions), but the **losses are high** if external rotor resistors are used continuously because $I^2R$ heat is dissipated in the resistors rather than recovered.
- The net effect: a **reduced speed with a "softer" torque-speed characteristic**. The operating point shifts to a lower speed on the same load line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept — The $V^2$ Dependency:**
> In the linear region of the torque-slip curve (small slip), the developed torque is directly proportional to the **square of the stator voltage**:
>
> $$T \propto \frac{s \cdot V_s^2}{R_2^2 + (sX_2)^2}$$
>
> This quadratic relationship is what allows fine control — halving the voltage gives **¼ of the torque**, which is dramatic.

## 1.3 Slip Power Recovery System (SPRS) — Intuitive Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
The *Slip Power Recovery System* is a method of recovering the otherwise-wasted slip power from the rotor of a slip-ring induction motor and feeding it back to the supply mains, thereby achieving **efficient sub-synchronous speed control**. It uses a **diode bridge rectifier** on the rotor side to convert the variable-frequency slip power to DC, which is then either fed back via a **line-commutated inverter** (Static Kramer / Scherbius Drive) or modulated via a **PWM chopper** (Static Scherbius with chopper).

**Intuitive Analogy: "The Hydroelectric Dam on the Drain Pipe"** 🌊

In the previous analogy, the water flowing past the rotor (the "slip") is just wasted friction. In SPRS, imagine installing a **mini hydroelectric turbine** on that drain pipe. Instead of wasting the rotor's "leftover" energy as heat, you convert it to electricity and **pump it back into the mains grid**.
- The rotor's slip frequency power is captured by a diode bridge (the "turbine").
- An inverter (the "pump-back station") then pushes this energy into the supply at line frequency.
- Result: **High efficiency speed control**, but at the cost of a complex power electronic circuit and poor power factor (in line-commutated versions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Two Principal Variants:**
> 1. **Static Kramer Drive** — recovers slip power at the *DC link* level and feeds back to the AC mains via an inverter. Provides **sub-synchronous speed control only**.
> 2. **Static Scherbius Drive** — uses a **DC motor** (rotary Scherbius) or a **chopper + inverter** (static Scherbius) to allow **sub-synchronous AND super-synchronous** speed control. The static version is the most exam-relevant.

## 1.4 Stepper Motor Drive — Intuitive Analogy

**Formal Definition:**
A *Stepper Motor* is a **brushless, synchronous electric motor** that converts digital pulses into discrete mechanical shaft rotations. Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular increment called the **step angle** $\beta$, which is determined by the number of stator/rotor poles. Stepper motors operate in an **open-loop** control system — the position is inferred from the number of pulses sent, no encoder required.

**Intuitive Analogy: "The Ratchet & Click Clock"** 🕰️

Imagine a clock's second hand: it doesn't glide — it *jumps* once per second. A stepper motor is the electromechanical equivalent.
- Send **1 pulse** → the shaft rotates **one step** (e.g., 1.8°).
- Send **100 pulses** → the shaft rotates **180°** (100 × 1.8°).
- **No feedback needed** (open-loop). The controller "trusts" that every pulse resulted in a step.
- The tradeoff: if the load torque exceeds the holding torque, the rotor *loses steps* and the position is lost — a phenomenon called **pull-out** or **slewing failure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Three Principal Types of Stepper Motors (KTU 2024 Focus):**
> 1. **Variable Reluctance (VR) Stepper Motor** — relies on the rotor's tendency to align with the path of minimum magnetic reluctance. No rotor magnet. High speed, no detent torque.
> 2. **Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor** — uses a permanently magnetized rotor. Has detent torque (holds position without excitation). Limited step resolution.
> 3. **Hybrid Stepper Motor** — combines both principles. Highest torque density, smallest step angles (down to **0.9°** or even **0.36°**), most widely used in industry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Controlled Slip System — Operational Logic

The circuit uses a **three-phase AC voltage controller** (anti-parallel SCR pairs) in each stator line.

### Step-by-Step Operational Logic:
- **Phase Angle Firing Control:** Each pair of anti-parallel thyristors is fired at a firing angle $\alpha$ measured from the zero-crossing of the supply voltage. As $\alpha$ increases, the RMS voltage $V_{rms}$ at the motor terminals decreases.
- **RMS Voltage Relationship:**
$$V_{rms} = V_{s} \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$$
  where $V_s$ is the supply RMS phase voltage and $\alpha$ is the firing angle ($0 < \alpha < \pi$).
- **Torque Reduction:** Since $T \propto V_{rms}^2$, a small increase in $\alpha$ produces a large reduction in developed torque. The load line intersects the new torque-slip curve at a **lower operating slip** → **lower rotor speed**.
- **Bidirectional Operation:** Because anti-parallel SCRs conduct on both half-cycles, the motor can be reversed by simply changing the phase sequence of the firing pulses (no mechanical contactor needed).

### Why the "Controlled Slip" Name?
The system's purpose is to **control the slip** (and hence the speed) of the induction motor by reducing the stator voltage. As load torque varies, the slip automatically adjusts to the new torque-speed curve — hence the "controlled slip" terminology.

### Real-World Utility:
- Used in **fan and pump drives** where torque requirement decreases with speed squared ($T_{load} \propto N^2$). A $2\times$ speed reduction requires $4\times$ less torque, perfectly matching the $V^2$ control law.
- **Crane and hoist applications** (brief low-speed operation during positioning).
- **Domestic appliance speed controls** (washing machines, mixer grinders).

## 2.2 Slip Power Recovery System — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 Slip Frequency & Power
For a slip-ring induction motor with slip $s$ and synchronous speed $N_s$:
- Rotor frequency: $f_r = s \cdot f_s$
- Rotor induced EMF per phase: $E_2 = s \cdot E_{20}$ (where $E_{20}$ is the rotor stand-still EMF)
- **Slip power** (total power crossing the air-gap that is not converted to mechanical work):
$$P_{slip} = s \cdot P_{ag} = 3 I_2^2 R_2 / s$$

In conventional rotor resistance control, this $P_{slip}$ is **dissipated as heat** in external resistors (low efficiency). In SPRS, this energy is **recovered to the supply**.

### 2.2.2 Static Kramer Drive
**Circuit Topology:**
`Rotor Slip Rings → 3-Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier → DC Link Inductor → Line-Commutated Inverter → AC Mains`

- The diode bridge converts the variable-frequency ($f_r = sf_s$) rotor EMF to a smooth DC voltage $V_d$.
- The line-commutated inverter (using SCRs) acts as a **DC-to-AC converter**, inverting at the supply frequency and pumping power back to the mains.
- **Speed Equation (Krammer):**
$$N = N_s (1 - s) = N_s \left[1 - \frac{V_{dc,inv}}{V_{d0}}\right]$$
  where $V_{dc,inv}$ is the average DC link voltage across the inverter and $V_{d0}$ is the no-load rectified rotor voltage (at $s = 1$).
- **Sub-synchronous operation only:** Power always flows from rotor → mains. The drive cannot operate above synchronous speed.

### 2.2.3 Static Scherbius Drive (Chopper-Controlled)
This is an enhancement that uses a **chopper + diode bridge** in place of the simple diode bridge, with a **DC link inductor** and an inverter. By varying the **chopper duty cycle $\delta$**, the effective rotor resistance seen by the motor is controlled.

- The chopper periodically short-circuits the rotor through an external resistance $R_{ext}$, with duty cycle $\delta$. The effective rotor resistance becomes:
$$R_{eff} = R_2 + (1-\delta)R_{ext}$$
- By adjusting $\delta$, smooth speed control is achieved.
- **Super-synchronous capability** is added by using a **rotating Scherbius** (with a DC machine on the same shaft), but in the KTU syllabus, the static Scherbius is **sub-synchronous only**.

## 2.3 Stepper Motor — Theory of Operation

### 2.3.1 Step Angle
The fundamental parameter is the **step angle** $\beta$:
$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N}$$
where $N$ is the total number of steps per revolution. For a motor with $P$ stator phase teeth and $R$ rotor teeth:
$$N = \frac{P \cdot R}{(\text{common factor})}$$
or for a multi-stack motor with $m$ stacks:
$$\beta = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$
where $N_r$ is the number of rotor teeth per stack.

### 2.3.2 Stepping Modes
- **Full-Step Mode (1-phase ON):** Only one phase is energized at a time. Lower torque, less heating.
- **Full-Step Mode (2-phase ON):** Two adjacent phases are energized. Higher torque (≈40% more than 1-phase).
- **Half-Step Mode:** Alternates between 1-phase and 2-phase ON. Doubles the resolution, smoothest motion at low speeds.
- **Microstepping:** The phase currents are modulated in fine sine-cosine envelopes, achieving up to **256 microsteps per full step**.

### 2.3.3 Torque-Speed Characteristics
Two critical torque parameters:
- **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** The maximum static torque the motor can hold with rated current in the energized phase(s) — without rotation.
- **Pull-Out (Slewing) Torque ($T_{po}$):** The maximum dynamic torque the motor can deliver at a given pulse rate without losing synchronism.

The torque-speed curve has two regions:
1. **Start-Stop Region:** Below the **pull-in torque curve** $T_{pi}$ — motor can start/stop/reverse at this rate.
2. **Slewing Region:** Between $T_{pi}$ and $T_{po}$ — motor can *run* at this rate but cannot start/stop without losing steps.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most exam-critical reference for Module 4. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Boundary / Units |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | RMS voltage of AC voltage controller | $V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$ | $0 \le \alpha \le \pi$; $V_s$ in Volts |
| 2 | Torque in linear region | $T = \frac{3}{\omega_s} \cdot \frac{s V_s^2 \cdot R_2/s}{(R_2/s)^2 + X_2^2}$ | Nm; $\omega_s$ in rad/s |
| 3 | Approx. torque-voltage relation | $T \propto V_s^2$ | Valid for small slip |
| 4 | Synchronous speed | $N_s = \frac{120 f}{P}$ | RPM; $f$ in Hz, $P$ = poles |
| 5 | Rotor frequency | $f_r = s f_s$ | Hz; $s$ = slip (per-unit) |
| 6 | Slip power | $P_{slip} = s P_{ag}$ | Watts |
| 7 | Kramer drive speed | $N = N_s\left(1 - \frac{V_{dc,inv}}{V_{d0}}\right)$ | RPM |
| 8 | Scherbius effective rotor R | $R_{eff} = R_2 + (1-\delta) R_{ext}$ | Ohms; $\delta$ = duty cycle |
| 9 | Step angle | $\beta = \frac{360°}{N}$ | Degrees |
| 10 | Step angle (multi-stack) | $\beta = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$ | Degrees |
| 11 | Electrical step angle | $\beta_e = \frac{360°}{N_r}$ (for VR/PM) | Degrees |
| 12 | Pull-out torque | $T_{po} \propto \sin(\text{load angle})$ | Nm |
| 13 | Stepping frequency to RPM | $N = f_{step} \cdot \beta \cdot \frac{60}{360} = \frac{f_{step} \cdot \beta}{6}$ | RPM |
| 14 | Resolution | $N_{res} = \frac{360°}{\beta}$ | Steps/rev |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of SPRS

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SPRS Matters in Industry:**
> - **Large pumps and compressors** (MW-range) where the load torque drops sharply with speed — a $20\%$ speed reduction saves $50\%$ power, and recovering slip power yields massive energy savings.
> - **Slip-ring induction motors in mines, cement mills, and hydro-pumped storage** are ideal candidates.
> - **Wind energy systems** use a variant of the Scherbius drive as a **static Scherbius / doubly-fed induction generator (DFIG)** topology.
> - Stepper motors dominate **CNC machines, 3D printers, robotics, and disk drives** for open-loop positioning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: AC Voltage Controller RMS Output Voltage

The most fundamental derivation for the Controlled Slip System is the relationship between the firing angle $\alpha$ and the RMS output voltage delivered to the motor.

### 3.1.1 Setup
Consider a single phase of the AC voltage controller with supply $v_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ and thyristor pair $T_1$ (positive half-cycle) and $T_2$ (negative half-cycle). Each thyristor fires at angle $\alpha$ after the respective zero-crossing.

### 3.1.2 RMS Voltage Computation
The RMS value is defined as:
$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T v_{out}^2(t) \, dt$$

Since the output is **symmetric** about the time axis, we only need to integrate over one positive half-cycle and double the result:
$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{2}{2\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} (V_m \sin \omega t)^2 \, d(\omega t)$$

$$\Rightarrow V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \sin^2 \omega t \, d(\omega t)$$

Applying the identity $\sin^2 \theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$:
$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2} \, d(\omega t)$$

Splitting the integral:
$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} d(\omega t) - \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos 2\omega t \, d(\omega t) \right]$$

Computing each piece:
- First integral: $\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} d(\omega t) = \pi - \alpha$
- Second integral: $\int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \cos 2\omega t \, d(\omega t) = \left[\frac{\sin 2\omega t}{2}\right]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{\sin 2\pi - \sin 2\alpha}{2} = -\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}$

Substituting back:
$$V_{rms}^2 = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) - \left(-\frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2}\right) \right] = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]$$

Therefore:
$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}$$

Since $V_s = V_m/\sqrt{2}$ (RMS supply voltage):
$$\boxed{V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - \frac{\alpha}{\pi} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2\pi}}}$$

**Verification of boundary conditions:**
- $\alpha = 0$ → $V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - 0 + 0} = V_s$ ✓ (full output)
- $\alpha = \pi$ → $V_{rms} = V_s \sqrt{1 - 1 + 0} = 0$ ✓ (no output)

## 3.2 Derivation: Step Angle for Multi-Stack VR Stepper Motor

Consider a **multi-stack variable reluctance stepper motor** with $m$ stacks and $N_r$ rotor teeth per stack. The stator of each stack has one phase winding that, when energized, attracts the nearest rotor tooth.

### Step-by-step reasoning:
- When phase A of stack 1 is energized, it aligns the rotor tooth at angular position $0°$.
- When phase A is de-energized and phase B is energized (in stack 1, displaced by one tooth pitch), the rotor rotates by **one tooth pitch** $\tau_r$:
$$\tau_r = \frac{360°}{N_r}$$
- Now consider the **multi-stack arrangement**: Stack 2 is mechanically displaced from Stack 1 by $\tau_r/m$. After one step in Stack 1, the tooth that aligns in Stack 1 is misaligned with Stack 2's energized phase by $\tau_r/m$, requiring an additional rotation of $\tau_r/m$ to align Stack 2.

**Total step angle:**
$$\beta = \frac{\tau_r}{m} = \frac{360°}{m \cdot N_r}$$

**Verification with example:** For a 3-stack VR motor with $N_r = 12$ rotor teeth:
$$\beta = \frac{360°}{3 \times 12} = 10° \text{ per step}$$
- Steps per revolution: $N = 360°/10° = 36$ steps/rev.

## 3.3 Derivation: Kramer Drive Speed Equation

The DC link in a Kramer drive has the rotor rectifier producing:
$$V_{d,rotor} = \frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \cdot s \cdot E_{20} - \frac{3 X_{r,slip}}{\pi} I_d$$
where $E_{20}$ is the rotor standstill EMF and $X_{r,slip} = sX_2$ is the slip-frequency rotor reactance. The line-commutated inverter imposes:
$$V_{d,inv} = \frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_L \cos \gamma - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$$
where $\gamma$ is the inverter firing angle advance and $X_c$ is the commutating reactance.

In steady state, $V_{d,rotor} = V_{d,inv}$. Neglecting commutation drops for simplicity:
$$\frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \cdot s \cdot E_{20} = \frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_L \cos \gamma$$

Solving for slip:
$$s = \frac{V_L \cos \gamma}{E_{20}}$$

Therefore, the operating speed is:
$$\boxed{N = N_s(1 - s) = N_s\left(1 - \frac{V_L \cos \gamma}{E_{20}}\right)}$$

**Key insight:** By varying the inverter firing angle $\gamma$ (or equivalently, varying $V_{d,inv}$), the slip $s$ — and hence the speed — is controlled.

## 3.4 Derivation: Static Scherbius with Chopper — Effective Rotor Resistance

A chopper switch $S$ with duty cycle $\delta$ connects an external resistance $R_{ext}$ across the rotor circuit. The chopper switches at high frequency.

- When the chopper is **ON** (duration $\delta T$): the external resistance is short-circuited; only $R_2$ is in the circuit.
- When the chopper is **OFF** (duration $(1-\delta)T$): current flows through $R_2 + R_{ext}$.

The **average power dissipated** in $R_{ext}$ per cycle is $I^2 R_{ext} (1-\delta)$. This is equivalent to a continuously present resistance of value $R_{ext,eff} = (1-\delta) R_{ext}$ (because average $I^2 R_{eff} = I^2 R_{ext} (1-\delta)$).

Therefore, the **effective rotor resistance** seen by the motor is:
$$\boxed{R_{eff} = R_2 + (1-\delta) R_{ext}}$$

This effective resistance is **continuously variable** from $R_2$ (when $\delta = 1$, chopper always ON) to $R_2 + R_{ext}$ (when $\delta = 0$, chopper always OFF). The motor thus behaves as if a **variable resistance** is in its rotor circuit — but with **far less power loss** than a true external resistor, because the chopper reduces the average current through $R_{ext}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of AC Motor Speed Control Methods

> [!NOTE]
> **This comparison matrix is the most common 14-mark question in KTU Module 4. Memorize the relative ordering of efficiency, complexity, and speed range.**

| Parameter | Stator Voltage Control (V-control) | Rotor Resistance Control | Static Kramer | Static Scherbius (Chopper) | V/f Control |
|-----------|------------------------------------|--------------------------|---------------|----------------------------|-------------|
| **Speed range** | Below synchronous | Below synchronous | Below synchronous | Below & above sync | Wide (below/above sync) |
| **Efficiency** | Low at low speed | Low at low speed | **High** (slip power recovered) | **High** | **High** |
| **Power factor** | Poor at low speed | Poor at low speed | Poor (line-commutated inv.) | Better with chopper | Good |
| **Smoothness** | Smooth (continuous) | Stepped (discrete R) | Smooth (continuous) | Smooth | Smooth |
| **Cost** | Low | Very low | High | High | Moderate |
| **Complexity** | Simple | Simple | Complex | Complex | Moderate |
| **Starting torque** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Good | Excellent |
| **Motor type** | Cage or wound | Wound (slip-ring) | Wound (slip-ring) | Wound (slip-ring) | Cage (preferred) |
| **Power rating** | Small/medium | Small/medium | Large (MW) | Large (MW) | Small to large |
| **Harmonics** | High | Low | High (rectifier + inverter) | High | Low (PWM) |

## 3.6 Derivation: Stepper Motor Synchronism Limit

For a stepper motor with rotor moment of inertia $J$ and viscous friction $B$, the maximum stepping rate $f_{max}$ for synchronous operation (no missed steps) is governed by the equation of motion:
$$J \frac{d^2 \theta}{dt^2} + B \frac{d\theta}{dt} + T_L = T_{em}(\theta)$$

For a single-step response, the rotor must complete one step in time $T = 1/f$. The maximum pull-out torque at frequency $f$ decreases with $f$ due to the winding inductance $L$ and rotor inertia.

A simplified first-order model gives the **maximum slewing frequency**:
$$f_{max} \approx \frac{V_{dc}}{2\pi L \cdot I_{rated}} \quad \text{(electrical limit)}$$
$$f_{max,mech} \approx \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{T_{po} - T_L}{J \beta}} \quad \text{(mechanical limit)}$$

The actual operating limit is the **smaller** of the electrical and mechanical limits. This is why stepper motors require **current-limiting chopper drives** at high stepping rates.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Phase AC Voltage Controller (Controlled Slip System)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[3-Phase AC Supply] -->|R Phase| B[T1a SCR]
    A -->|R Phase| B
    B --> C[T1b SCR Anti-parallel]
    C --> D[R Phase to Motor]
    A -->|Y Phase| E[T2a SCR]
    E --> F[T2b SCR Anti-parallel]
    F --> G[Y Phase to Motor]
    A -->|B Phase| H[T3a SCR]
    H --> I[T3b SCR Anti-parallel]
    I --> J[B Phase to Motor]
    D --> K[Induction Motor Stator]
    G --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L{Rotor: Cage or Wound}
    L --> M[Variable R External]
    L --> N[Short Circuit]
    M --> O[Speed Control Output]
    N --> O
```

**Description:** The 3-phase AC voltage controller consists of **6 thyristors** arranged in 3 anti-parallel pairs, one per phase. The firing circuit (not shown) generates gate pulses at angle $\alpha$ after each zero-crossing. The motor's rotor can be either short-circuited (cage-like behavior with stator voltage control) or connected to a variable external resistance $R_{ext}$.

## 4.2 Static Kramer Drive — Full Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[3-Phase AC Mains] -->|V_L| B[Slip-Ring Induction Motor Stator]
    B --> C[Rotor Slip Rings s E20]
    C --> D[3-Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier]
    D -->|V_d DC| E[DC Link Inductor L_d]
    E --> F[DC Link Capacitor C_d Optional]
    F --> G[Line-Commutated Thyristor Inverter]
    G -->|Feedback Power| A
    H[Firing Circuit for Inverter] -->|Gamma Control| G
    H -->|Alpha Control| I[Stator Voltage Controller]
    I --> B
```

**Description:** Power flow in the Static Kramer Drive:
1. The rotor's variable-frequency AC ($f_r = sf_s$) is rectified by the diode bridge to DC.
2. The DC link inductor smooths the current.
3. The line-commutated inverter converts DC back to AC at supply frequency and pumps it back to the mains.
4. Speed is controlled by the **inverter firing angle $\gamma$** — increasing $\gamma$ reduces $V_{d,inv}$, increases the operating slip, and reduces the motor speed.

## 4.3 Static Scherbius Drive with Chopper Controller

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[3-Phase AC Mains] -->|V_L| B[Slip-Ring Induction Motor Stator]
    B --> C[Rotor Slip Rings]
    C --> D[3-Phase Diode Bridge]
    D -->|V_d| E[DC Link Inductor L]
    E --> F[IGBT Chopper Switch]
    F -->|Duty Cycle delta| G[External Resistor R_ext]
    F --> H[Inverter SCR Bridge]
    H -->|Power Recovery| A
    I[Chopper Control PWM] --> F
    J[Inverter Firing Gamma] --> H
    B -.-> I
```

**Description:** The chopper with duty cycle $\delta$ provides smooth, continuous control of effective rotor resistance, while the inverter maintains the DC link at the desired voltage. The combination enables:
- **Sub-synchronous operation** (rotor power → mains, like Kramer)
- **Improved power factor** (chopper can be operated at leading $\gamma$ to compensate)
- **Lower harmonic content** at the supply side.

## 4.4 Stepper Motor Drive — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Microcontroller or PC] -->|Step Pulses| B[Pulse Generator and Direction Logic]
    B -->|Step Signal| C[Ring Counter and Translator]
    C -->|Phase A Pulses| D1[Driver A]
    C -->|Phase B Pulses| D2[Driver B]
    C -->|Phase C Pulses| D3[Driver C]
    C -->|Phase D Pulses| D4[Driver D]
    D1 --> E1[Phase A Winding]
    D2 --> E2[Phase B Winding]
    D3 --> E3[Phase C Winding]
    D4 --> E4[Phase D Winding]
    E1 --> F[Stepper Motor Rotor]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    G[DC Power Supply] --> D1
    G --> D2
    G --> D3
    G --> D4
    F -->|Mechanical Output| H[Shaft Position theta]
```

**Description:** The drive system consists of:
- **Pulse Source:** A microcontroller or pulse generator producing a variable-frequency square wave.
- **Ring Counter / Translator:** Decodes the step pulses into the correct phase energization sequence (e.g., A → AB → B → BC → C → CD → D → DA for a 4-phase full-step sequence).
- **Drivers:** Power amplifiers (transistor or MOSFET H-bridges) that switch the high-current phase windings. Modern drives use **constant-current chopper drivers** for high-speed performance.
- **Motor:** Multi-phase stepper motor (2-phase bipolar for PM/hybrid, 3- or 4-phase unipolar for VR).

## 4.5 Hybrid Stepper Motor — Cross-Sectional Concept

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Stator Phase A Pole with Teeth] --> B[Air Gap]
    B --> C[Rotor Tooth 1 Aligned with Stator A Tooth]
    A2[Stator Phase B Pole offset by 1.8 degrees] --> B2[Air Gap 2]
    B2 --> C2[Rotor Tooth 2 offset by 1.8 degrees]
    D[Permanent Magnet Axial] --> C
    D --> C2
```

**Description:** A hybrid stepper motor has a **two-stack rotor** with an axial permanent magnet. The two rotor stacks are offset by **half a tooth pitch**. Each stator phase, when energized, aligns one rotor stack while misaligning the other. Switching the phase causes a **half-tooth-pitch rotation** — yielding very small step angles (typically **0.9° or 1.8°**).

## 4.6 Stepper Motor Phase Excitation Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    S0[Step 0 Phase A ON] --> S1[Step 1 Phase AB ON]
    S1 --> S2[Step 2 Phase B ON]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Phase BC ON]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Phase C ON]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Phase CD ON]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Phase D ON]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7 Phase DA ON]
    S7 --> S0
```

**Description:** This is the **full-step 2-phase-ON sequence** for a 4-phase stepper motor. Each transition causes a **one full step** rotation. The half-step sequence alternates between 1-phase-ON and 2-phase-ON states, doubling the resolution. Reverse direction is achieved by simply traversing the sequence in reverse.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Static Kramer Drive — Concept

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Slip Power Recovery System. With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of the Static Kramer Drive.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Slip Power Recovery System (SPRS)** is a method of controlling the speed of a slip-ring induction motor by recovering the slip power from the rotor circuit and feeding it back to the AC mains, instead of dissipating it as heat in external rotor resistors. This improves the overall efficiency of sub-synchronous speed control.

**Block Diagram Operation (Static Kramer Drive):**

The system consists of:
1. **3-Phase Slip-Ring Induction Motor:** The stator is connected directly to the AC supply; the rotor delivers variable-frequency AC at slip frequency $f_r = sf_s$.
2. **3-Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier:** Converts the rotor's variable-frequency AC to a smooth DC voltage $V_{d,rotor} = \frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} s E_{20}$.
3. **DC Link Inductor:** Smooths the DC link current and provides a stiff current source for the inverter.
4. **Line-Commutated Inverter (SCR Bridge):** Converts the DC power back to AC at supply frequency, with DC link voltage $V_{d,inv} = \frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_L \cos \gamma$.
5. **Firing Circuit:** Controls the inverter firing angle $\gamma$ (and optionally a stator voltage controller for starting).

**Speed Equation:** $N = N_s\left(1 - \frac{V_L \cos \gamma}{E_{20}}\right)$

**Advantages:** High efficiency, smooth speed control, sub-synchronous speed range.
**Disadvantages:** Poor power factor, requires slip-ring motor, complex circuitry, harmonics injected to the mains.

---

### Question 2: Stepper Motor — Definition and Application

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] What is a stepper motor? List any two industrial applications and state the two torque parameters used to characterize its performance.**

**Model Answer:**

A **stepper motor** is a brushless, synchronous electric motor that converts a series of digital electrical pulses into discrete angular mechanical movements (steps). Each pulse advances the rotor by a fixed angular increment called the **step angle** $\beta$. It operates in an **open-loop** mode — no feedback sensor is required for position control.

**Two Industrial Applications:**
1. **Computer Numeric Control (CNC) machines** for X-Y-Z axis positioning of cutting tools.
2. **3D Printers** for precise filament head movement and extruder control.
   *(Other valid: robotics joint control, hard disk drive head positioning, textile machines, plotter drives.)*

**Two Torque Parameters:**
1. **Holding Torque ($T_h$):** The maximum torque the motor can hold stationary with rated current flowing through the energized phase(s). Measured with the rotor locked and one or more phases energized.
2. **Pull-Out (or Slewing) Torque ($T_{po}$):** The maximum torque the motor can deliver at a given pulse rate while still maintaining synchronism (i.e., without losing steps). This torque decreases as the stepping frequency increases.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Common marks-loss areas in Module 4:
> 1. **Forgetting units** in derivations (slip is dimensionless; speed in RPM; torque in N·m).
> 2. **Drawing the circuit topology incorrectly** — confusing Kramer (rotor + diode bridge + inverter) with Scherbius (chopper between diode bridge and inverter).
> 3. **Mixing up the slip power equation** $P_{slip} = s P_{ag}$ with the air-gap power $P_{ag}$. Students often write $P_{slip} = (1-s) P_{ag}$ (which is the mechanical power developed, NOT the slip power).
> 4. **Stepper motor step angle** — confusing the *electrical step angle* (one tooth pitch) with the *mechanical step angle* (rotor rotation per pulse). For multi-stack motors, these are different.
> 5. **Not labeling the firing angles** $\alpha$, $\gamma$, and duty cycle $\delta$ clearly in diagrams.

---

### PART B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]: With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of the Static Kramer Drive for speed control of a slip-ring induction motor. Derive the expression for the motor speed in terms of the inverter firing angle.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram (textual representation since this is a written exam):**
```
3-Phase Mains ──► Stator of Slip-Ring IM ── (direct connection)
                          ▲
                          │
                   Rotor Slip Rings
                          │
                          ▼
              3-Phase Diode Bridge Rectifier (D1-D6)
                          │
                          ▼
                    DC Link Inductor L_d
                          │
                          ▼
                Line-Commutated Inverter (SCRs T1-T6)
                          │
                          ▼
                    3-Phase AC Mains (Feedback)
```

**Operation:**

The slip-ring induction motor's stator is connected directly to the 3-phase AC supply at voltage $V_L$ and frequency $f_s$. The rotor, rotating at slip $s$ relative to the synchronous speed $N_s$, has induced EMF $sE_{20}$ at frequency $sf_s$.

This variable-frequency rotor EMF is fed to a 3-phase uncontrolled diode bridge rectifier. The rectifier produces an average DC output voltage:
$$V_{d,rotor} = \frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} \cdot s \cdot E_{20} - \frac{3 s X_2}{\pi} I_d$$

where $E_{20}$ is the rotor standstill EMF, $X_2$ is the rotor reactance at standstill, and $I_d$ is the DC link current. The DC link inductor $L_d$ smooths the current.

The line-commutated inverter (using 6 SCRs in a 3-phase bridge configuration) inverts this DC power back to AC at the supply frequency. The inverter's average DC terminal voltage is:
$$V_{d,inv} = \frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_L \cos \gamma - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$$

where $\gamma$ is the inverter firing angle advance (from the commutation margin) and $X_c$ is the commutating reactance. For self-commutation of the inverter, $\gamma < 90°$ is required (otherwise the inverter's DC voltage becomes positive, meaning it acts as a rectifier).

**Derivation of Speed Equation:**

In steady state, $V_{d,rotor} = V_{d,inv}$:
$$\frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} s E_{20} - \frac{3 s X_2}{\pi} I_d = \frac{3\sqrt{6}}{\pi} V_L \cos \gamma - \frac{3 X_c}{\pi} I_d$$

Neglecting commutation drops ($I_d$ terms cancel approximately when $X_c \approx sX_2$):
$$s E_{20} \approx V_L \cos \gamma$$
$$s = \frac{V_L \cos \gamma}{E_{20}}$$

The motor speed is:
$$N = N_s(1 - s) = N_s\left(1 - \frac{V_L \cos \gamma}{E_{20}}\right)$$

**Speed Control:** By varying the inverter firing angle $\gamma$ from $0°$ to about $80°$, the slip $s$ varies from 0 to nearly 1, and the speed varies from $N_s$ to nearly zero. **Sub-synchronous operation only** because $s > 0$ is required (power flows from rotor to mains, not vice versa).

---

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]: Explain with a block diagram the operation of a closed-loop speed control scheme for a stepper motor. State its advantages over open-loop control.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of Closed-Loop Stepper Motor Control:**

```
  Reference ──► Comparator ──► Phase Sequencer ──► Driver ──► Stepper Motor ──► Load
  Pulses (+)         ▲              │              │           │
   (-) ◄── Feedback Encoder ────────┘              │           │
                                                  │           │
                                            Current Feedback  │
                                                  │           │
                                                  └───────────┘
```

**Detailed Operation:**

1. **Reference Pulse Generator:** Produces a train of pulses corresponding to the desired step count and direction.
2. **Comparator:** Compares the reference pulse count with the feedback pulse count from the encoder mounted on the motor shaft.
3. **Error Generator:** If the motor has lost steps (encoder pulse count < reference), the comparator generates an error signal that is fed back to the phase sequencer to advance the motor by the missed steps.
4. **Phase Sequencer / Translator:** Decodes the (corrected) step pulses into the proper phase energization sequence (A → AB → B → BC → ...).
5. **Driver:** Power amplifier (chopper-type constant current) that drives the phase windings with the commanded current waveform.
6. **Stepper Motor and Load:** Converts the electrical pulses into mechanical rotation.
7. **Encoder:** An optical or magnetic encoder on the motor shaft provides the actual position feedback to the comparator.

**Advantages of Closed-Loop Control over Open-Loop:**

1. **Detection and correction of lost steps:** If the load torque momentarily exceeds the pull-out torque, the motor skips steps. Open-loop systems have no way to detect this, leading to **positioning errors**. Closed-loop systems detect the discrepancy and apply corrective pulses.
2. **Higher reliable operating speed:** The motor can be operated closer to the pull-out torque limit because the closed-loop system compensates for momentary overloads.
3. **Better dynamic response:** Acceleration/deceleration profiles can be precisely implemented because the actual position is always known.
4. **Improved safety in critical applications** (medical, aerospace): position is verified continuously.

**Disadvantage:** Additional cost and complexity of the encoder and feedback electronics, which somewhat negates the primary advantage of stepper motors (inherent open-loop position control).

---

### PART B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Model Question Paper, Module 4]**

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the Static Scherbius Drive with a chopper controller. Derive the expression for the effective rotor resistance.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:**

The Static Scherbius Drive with chopper controller uses the same fundamental topology as the Kramer drive, but with a **chopper switch** inserted between the diode bridge output and the DC link inductor. The chopper periodically short-circuits the rotor circuit through a fixed external resistance $R_{ext}$, with a controllable duty cycle $\delta$.

```
  Rotor Slip Rings ──► 3-Phase Diode Bridge ──► Chopper Switch S (IGBT) ──► DC Link Inductor L
                                              │                                                       │
                                              └── R_ext (in parallel with chopper) ───────────────────┘
                                                                                                       │
                                                                                                       ▼
                                                                                              Line-Commutated Inverter
                                                                                                       │
                                                                                                       ▼
                                                                                              3-Phase AC Mains
```

**Operation:**

The diode bridge rectifies the rotor's variable-frequency AC to a pulsating DC voltage. The chopper switch $S$ (typically an IGBT) operates at high frequency (e.g., 1–5 kHz) with duty cycle:
$$\delta = \frac{T_{on}}{T_{on} + T_{off}}$$

When $S$ is ON, the external resistance $R_{ext}$ is short-circuited, and the rotor current flows only through the rotor's own resistance $R_2$. When $S$ is OFF, the rotor current must flow through $R_{ext}$ in addition to $R_2$.

**Derivation of Effective Rotor Resistance:**

The **average power dissipated** in $R_{ext}$ over one chopper cycle is:
$$P_{R_{ext}} = I^2 R_{ext} (1 - \delta)$$

where $I$ is the RMS rotor current (assumed constant over one chopper cycle since the chopper frequency is much higher than the slip frequency).

If an equivalent continuous resistance $R_{ext,eff}$ is imagined, then:
$$I^2 R_{ext,eff} = I^2 R_{ext} (1 - \delta)$$
$$R_{ext,eff} = R_{ext} (1 - \delta)$$

The total effective rotor resistance seen by the motor is therefore:
$$\boxed{R_{eff} = R_2 + (1 - \delta) R_{ext}}$$

**Range of Control:**
- $\delta = 1$ (chopper always ON): $R_{eff} = R_2$ (minimum rotor resistance, maximum speed)
- $\delta = 0$ (chopper always OFF): $R_{eff} = R_2 + R_{ext}$ (maximum rotor resistance, minimum speed)

**Advantages over Plain Rotor Resistance Control:**
1. The chopper reduces the **average current** through $R_{ext}$, lowering the $I^2R$ loss.
2. Speed control is **continuous and smooth** (not stepped as in discrete resistance switching).
3. The chopper can be used for **regenerative braking** by reversing the power flow direction.

---

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]: With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a Permanent Magnet (PM) Stepper Motor. State two advantages and two disadvantages of the hybrid stepper motor over the PM stepper motor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction of PM Stepper Motor:**

The Permanent Magnet Stepper Motor consists of:
1. **Stator:** A multi-toothed soft-iron stator with **two or four salient pole pairs**, each carrying a concentrated field winding. The stator is the same as that of a VR or hybrid motor.
2. **Rotor:** A cylindrical permanent magnet (typically rare-earth, e.g., samarium-cobalt or neodymium-iron-boron) **axially magnetized**, with two pole pairs. The rotor poles are aligned along the axis of the shaft.
3. **End Caps / Pole Pieces:** Soft-iron pole pieces are mounted on the rotor's ends to provide salient poles that interact with the stator's teeth.

**Working Principle:**

When a stator phase (e.g., Phase A) is energized, the stator pole becomes a magnetic pole of opposite polarity to the nearest rotor pole. The rotor's PM aligns itself such that the **unlike poles face each other** (attraction). When Phase A is de-energized and Phase B is energized, the rotor rotates by the angle between the two adjacent stator pole positions (one step).

For a 2-phase, 4-pole PM motor with 50 stator teeth and 50 rotor teeth, the **step angle** is:
$$\beta = \frac{360°}{N_s \cdot p} = \frac{360°}{50 \times 2} = 3.6° \text{ per step}$$

(where $N_s$ is the number of stator teeth and $p$ is the number of pole pairs).

**PM Motor Step Angle (general formula):**
$$\beta = \frac{180°}{p \cdot N_r}$$
where $p$ = number of stator pole pairs and $N_r$ = number of rotor teeth.

**Holding Torque (Detent Torque):**
The PM rotor produces a **detent torque** even when no phase is energized. This is the torque required to displace the rotor from its rest position. It is due to the rotor's PM trying to align with the stator's salient poles. This detent torque helps the motor **hold position** during de-energized states — a key advantage of PM steppers over VR steppers.

**Comparison: Hybrid vs. PM Stepper Motor**

| Feature | PM Stepper | Hybrid Stepper |
|---------|-----------|----------------|
| Step resolution (typical) | 7.5°, 15°, 30° (coarse) | 0.9°, 1.8° (fine) |
| Detent torque | High | Moderate |
| Holding torque | Moderate | High |
| Speed range | Low to medium | Medium to high |
| Construction | Simpler | More complex (multi-stack) |
| Cost | Low | Higher |
| Rotor inertia | Lower | Higher |
| Step accuracy | ±5% (typical) | ±3% (better) |

**Two Advantages of Hybrid over PM:**
1. **Finer step resolution:** Hybrid motors can achieve step angles of 0.9° or smaller, while PM motors are typically limited to 7.5° or larger. This is critical for high-precision applications.
2. **Higher torque-to-inertia ratio:** Hybrid motors deliver more torque for a given rotor size, enabling faster acceleration and higher slewing rates.

**Two Disadvantages of Hybrid over PM:**
1. **Higher cost and manufacturing complexity** due to the multi-stack rotor and precisely machined teeth.
2. **Higher rotor inertia** (because of the additional soft-iron pole pieces), which limits the maximum acceleration and slewing rate at high stepping frequencies.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4 (AC Motor Control)**

- [x] **Controlled Slip System** = stator voltage control using AC-AC phase-controlled regulators (6 thyristors in 3 anti-parallel pairs). Torque $\propto V^2$, suitable for fan/pump loads. Bidirectional.
- [x] **Slip Power Recovery System (SPRS)** = recovers rotor slip power and feeds it back to the AC mains, achieving efficient sub-synchronous speed control.
- [x] **Static Kramer Drive** = 3-phase diode bridge + DC link inductor + line-commutated inverter. Speed equation: $N = N_s(1 - V_L \cos\gamma / E_{20})$. **Sub-synchronous only.**
- [x] **Static Scherbius Drive** = diode bridge + chopper + inverter. Effective rotor resistance: $R_{eff} = R_2 + (1-\delta) R_{ext}$. Lower losses than discrete rotor resistance control.
- [x] **Slip power** = $P_{slip} = s \cdot P_{ag}$ (NOT $(1-s)P_{ag}$ — that's mechanical power developed).
- [x] **Stepper Motor** = brushless, open-loop, digital-to-mechanical converter. Step angle $\beta = 360°/N$.
- [x] **Three types:** VR (no magnet, lowest detent torque), PM (permanent magnet rotor, good detent torque), **Hybrid (smallest step angle, highest torque, most widely used)**.
- [x] **Step angle formulas:** $\beta = 360°/N$ (general), $\beta = 360°/(m \cdot N_r)$ (multi-stack VR), $\beta = 180°/(p \cdot N_r)$ (PM).
- [x] **Two critical torques:** Holding torque $T_h$ (static, locked rotor) and Pull-out torque $T_{po}$ (dynamic, slewing). $T_{po} > T_h$.
- [x] **Two stepping regions:** Pull-in region (start/stop below $T_{pi}$ curve) and slewing region (running between $T_{pi}$ and $T_{po}$).
- [x] **Open-loop problem:** lost steps if load torque exceeds $T_{po}$. Closed-loop with encoder adds error correction.
- [x] **Microstepping** modulates phase currents in fine sine-cosine envelopes, achieving up to 256 microsteps per full step for ultra-smooth motion.
- [x] **Examiner's favorite comparison:** Kramer vs. Scherbius; PM vs. Hybrid stepper; Voltage control vs. SPRS efficiency.
- [x] **Boundary conditions to remember:** $s=0$ at synchronous, $s=1$ at standstill. Inverter must have $\gamma < 90°$ for inversion mode. Chopper $\delta$ ranges from 0 to 1.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UPS and Battery Charging Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT636 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** UPS and Battery Charging Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Textbook Terminology):**
> An **Uninterruptible Power Supply (UPS)** is a power-electronics-based power conditioning system that provides **continuous, regulated, and instantaneous backup power** to a critical load during mains failure, voltage sag, surge, frequency drift, or total blackout. It employs an energy-storage element (typically a **battery bank**) coupled through a **rectifier–inverter** chain to deliver clean sinusoidal AC output without any transfer break (online type) or with a minimal transfer time (offline/line-interactive type).

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the UPS as a **"Power Insurance Policy with a Generators Backup."**
- Your home electricity is like a river feeding your city. Sometimes the river dries up (blackout), sometimes it floods (surge), sometimes it gets polluted (noise/harmonics).
- The UPS is a **water tank on your roof with an automatic pump**. When the river (mains) is healthy, the pump (rectifier/charger) keeps the tank (battery) full and simultaneously supplies your house.
- The instant the river stops, a **valve (transfer switch)** opens and the tank water (DC battery) is pushed through a **purifier (inverter)** to give you clean, pressurized water (pure sine wave AC) for your essentials.
- You never even notice the river stopped — because the tank was always full and ready.

### 1.1.2 Why UPS is Critical in Modern Engineering

| Application Domain | Why UPS is Mandatory |
|---|---|
| Data Centers & Servers | A 10 ms interruption can crash databases and corrupt terabytes of data |
| Hospitals (ICUs, OTs) | Life-support equipment cannot tolerate any power break |
| Industrial PLCs & SCADA | Process controllers may enter safe-stop, ruining batch production |
| Telecom Towers | Base stations must remain on-air 24×7 for emergency services |
| Semiconductor Fabs | A single microsecond glitch can destroy a wafer worth **₹10,00,000+** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** A 2-mark direct question on "List and briefly explain the three topologies of UPS" is a **frequently repeated question** in Part A. Students must memorize the **transfer time** differences.

## 1.2 Classification of UPS Systems (KTU Board Favourite)

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly categorizes UPS into three functional classes as per **IEC 62040-3** standards:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three standard UPS classifications are:
> 1. **Passive Standby (Offline / VFD — Voltage & Frequency Dependent)**
> 2. **Line Interactive (VI — Voltage Independent)**
> 3. **Double Conversion Online (VFI — Voltage & Frequency Independent)**

### 1.2.1 Type 1 — Offline UPS (Passive Standby / VFD)

**Definition:** The load is normally fed **directly from the raw mains** through a passive filter. The inverter and battery sit *idle*. Only when mains fails (or sags beyond ±20%) does a **static transfer switch (STS)** commutate the load to the inverter output.

**Transfer Time:** Typically **4 to 8 milliseconds (ms)** — acceptable for PCs, printers, and home routers, **NOT** acceptable for sensitive servers.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Like a **standby generator** at a wedding hall — it kicks in *only* when the main power fails, with a tiny delay.

### 1.2.2 Type 2 — Line-Interactive UPS (VI)

**Definition:** The mains path contains a **bidirectional autotransformer (or tapped inductor)** controlled by a **thyristor-grade tap-changer or IGBT buck/boost stage**. The inverter *runs continuously* in parallel to provide **active regulation** of voltage, but only takes over the full load on mains failure.

**Transfer Time:** Typically **2 to 4 ms** — better than offline, with built-in voltage regulation.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Like a **voltage stabilizer (Servo)** for your TV — it continuously *adjusts* the voltage up or down, and only switches to battery when input goes completely dead.

### 1.2.3 Type 3 — Online UPS (Double Conversion / VFI)

**Definition:** The load is **never** connected to raw mains. Power flows **Mains → Rectifier → DC Bus → Inverter → Load** continuously. The battery is permanently *floating* on the DC bus. On mains failure, the rectifier shuts down and the battery seamlessly feeds the inverter — **zero transfer time**.

**Transfer Time:** **0 ms (True Online)** — the gold standard for mission-critical loads.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Like driving a **hybrid car in EV mode** — the petrol engine (mains) only charges the battery; the wheels (load) are *always* driven by the electric motor (inverter). You never feel a switchover.

## 1.3 Battery Charging Techniques — Formal Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** Battery charging is the controlled process of **restoring electrochemical energy** to a secondary (rechargeable) cell by forcing a **DC current/voltage profile** that follows the battery's internal chemistry. Power electronics converters (SCR rectifiers, SMPS chargers, buck/boost converters) are used to realize these profiles.

### 1.3.1 Why Smart Charging is an Engineering Necessity

A Lead-Acid cell has a nominal voltage of **2 V/cell (Nominal Energy Density ~30–40 Wh/kg)**. If you over-charge it above **2.4 V/cell**, it begins **gassing** (electrolysis of water → H₂ + O₂), losing electrolyte. If you under-charge it, **sulfation** (PbSO₄ crystals) builds up on plates, permanently reducing capacity. Hence, the **charging profile must be chemistry-aware**.

### 1.3.2 The Four Standard Charging Methods (KTU Syllabus)

| # | Method | One-line Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Constant Current (CC) Charging** | A fixed DC current is forced into the battery regardless of its terminal voltage |
| 2 | **Constant Voltage (CV) Charging** | A fixed DC voltage is applied; current tapers naturally as the battery reaches full charge |
| 3 | **Trickle / Float Charging** | A very small current is supplied to compensate for self-discharge and keep the battery at **100% SOC** |
| 4 | **Pulse / Reflex Charging** | Short charging current pulses are interspersed with brief discharge pulses to desulfate plates and equalize cells |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition Box — "Feeding a Hungry Child"**
> - **CC** is like giving the child *one spoonful every minute* — slow but safe.
> - **CV** is like *pouring water into a glass that is already full* — it just overflows if you pour too fast, so the flow naturally slows.
> - **Trickle** is like *letting the child sip from a straw* throughout the day to stay full.
> - **Pulse** is like *gulping a big drink, then burping (discharge pulse)*, then gulping again — used to break up gas bubbles.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Architecture of the Three UPS Topologies

### 2.1.1 Offline UPS — Block-by-Block Operation

**Power Path (Mains Healthy State):**
- Mains AC → **Surge Suppressor (MOV)** → **EMI/RFI Filter (LC)** → **Static Transfer Switch (STS)** → Load
- Simultaneously: Mains AC → **Battery Charger (SCR/SMPS rectifier)** → Battery Bank
- Inverter is in **standby** (cold); STS connects load to filtered mains.

**Power Path (Mains Failure):**
- Charger detects mains loss within **<4 ms**
- STS opens the mains contact
- Inverter is cold-started from battery (PWM IGBT full-bridge)
- STS closes the inverter contact to feed the load
- Total transfer break ≈ **4–8 ms**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Offline UPS has a transfer break:** The IGBTs of the inverter are not switching during standby (to save losses). On mains failure, the control circuit must first *detect* the loss, *soft-start* the inverter, *synchronize* the output, and *commutate* the STS. Each of these steps takes time — hence the unavoidable break.

### 2.1.2 Online (Double Conversion) UPS — Continuous Power Path

**Normal Operation:**
- Mains AC → **PFC Rectifier (Boost-type, IGBT)** → **DC Bus (≈ 400 V for 230 V output)** → **Inverter (IGBT full-bridge, SPWM/SVPWM)** → **Output Filter (LC)** → Load
- Battery is connected to DC bus via a **buck-boost bidirectional converter** — it is *always* floating at the DC-link voltage
- Inverter continuously synthesizes a **pure sine wave**, totally decoupled from mains

**Mains Failure Operation:**
- PFC Rectifier stops drawing from mains (input current → 0 A)
- Battery bidirectional converter starts *discharging* into the DC bus
- DC-bus voltage is **regulated to remain constant** (e.g., 400 V ± 1%)
- The load sees **zero disturbance** — true "no-break" operation

> [!TIP]
> **Why Online UPS is the Premium Choice:** Because the inverter *always* feeds the load, any mains glitch (sag, surge, spike, frequency drift, harmonic distortion) is **completely isolated** by the rectifier's boost PFC stage. The load is effectively connected to a **"clean power island."**

### 2.1.3 Line-Interactive UPS — The Hybrid Design

**Power Path (Mains Healthy):**
- Mains → **Bidirectional Inductor / Autotransformer** (with **IGBT tap-switching** for buck/boost) → STS → Load
- Inverter runs in **active-parallel mode** — it injects/exacts reactive power to regulate voltage and corrects power factor

**Power Path (Mains Failure):**
- STS disconnects mains
- Inverter takes **full load** (no longer just compensating)
- Transfer time ≈ **2–4 ms**

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** Line-Interactive UPS is **cheaper** than Online (only one power stage is fully rated), but **more efficient** than Offline (inverter is always warm, no cold-start). It is the *sweet spot* for **small offices, ATMs, and network switches**.

## 2.2 Battery Chemistry Used in UPS (KTU Exam Frequently Tested)

| Battery Type | Nominal V/cell | Energy Density | Cycle Life | Self-Discharge | Typical UPS Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **VRLA (Valve-Regulated Lead-Acid)** | **2 V** | 30–40 Wh/kg | 200–500 | 3–20 %/month | Most common in small/medium UPS |
| **Flooded Lead-Acid (Tubular)** | **2 V** | 25–35 Wh/kg | 1000–1500 | 4–15 %/month | Large industrial UPS, telecom |
| **Lithium-Ion (LiFePO₄)** | **3.2 V** | 90–160 Wh/kg | 2000–5000 | 1–3 %/month | Modern premium UPS, data centers |
| **Ni-Cd** | **1.2 V** | 40–60 Wh/kg | 1500–2000 | 10–20 %/month | Harsh-environment UPS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Mark-worthy Point:** Always state the nominal cell voltage and the **number of cells in series** to achieve a target DC-bus voltage. For example, a 48 V DC bus (telecom) uses **24 × 2 V VRLA cells**; a 384 V DC bus (online UPS) uses **192 × 2 V cells** or **96 × 12 V monoblocs**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **all** high-yield equations for Module 4. Memorize them with units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable To |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{batt} = V \times I \times t$ | Stored energy | Wh | Battery sizing |
| 2 | $E_{batt} = \dfrac{P_{load} \times T_{backup}}{\eta_{inv}}$ | Required battery energy | Wh | UPS design |
| 3 | $T_{backup} = \dfrac{C_{Ah} \times V_{batt}}{P_{load}} \times \eta_{overall}$ | Backup time | hours (h) | UPS design |
| 4 | $I_{charge(CC)} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{T_{charge}}$ | Constant charging current | A | CC charging |
| 5 | $V_{float} = n_{cells} \times 2.25$ V | Float voltage per cell for VRLA | V | Float charging |
| 6 | $V_{boost} = n_{cells} \times 2.40$ V | Boost/equalization voltage | V | Equalization charging |
| 7 | $V_{cutoff} = n_{cells} \times 1.75$ V | End-of-discharge voltage (DoD = 100 %) | V | Battery discharge |
| 8 | $\eta_{charge} = \dfrac{E_{discharged}}{E_{charged}} \times 100\,\%$ | Round-trip Coulombic efficiency | % | Battery health |
| 9 | $SOC(t) = SOC_0 + \dfrac{1}{C_{Ah}} \int_{0}^{t} I_{charge}(\tau)\,d\tau$ | State of Charge evolution | % | Battery management |
| 10 | $P_{out,inverter} = \sqrt{3} \times V_{L} \times I_{L} \times \cos\phi$ | Three-phase inverter output | W | Inverter sizing |
| 11 | $V_{DC,bus} \geq \sqrt{2} \times V_{L,rms}$ | Minimum DC-link voltage | V | Inverter design |
| 12 | $THD = \dfrac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{N} V_n^2}}{V_1} \times 100\,\%$ | Output voltage distortion | % | Output quality |
| 13 | $I_{ripple,rms} = I_{DC} \times \sqrt{2 \times D \times (1-D)}$ | Buck-converter ripple current | A | Charger design |
| 14 | $f_{switch} = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | SMPS switching frequency | Hz | High-freq charger |
| 15 | $C_{bulk} \geq \dfrac{I_{load} \times \Delta t}{\Delta V_{DC}}$ | Hold-up capacitor sizing | F | DC-bus design |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Equation 2 is the *backbone* of UPS sizing problems in KTU ESE Part B questions. A 14-mark question typically gives you the load (kW), required backup (min), and inverter efficiency, and asks you to size the battery bank (number of cells, Ah rating, charging current).

## 2.4 Detailed Comparison Table (Favourite 7-Mark Sub-Question)

| Parameter | Offline (VFD) | Line-Interactive (VI) | Online (VFI) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mains-to-Load path | Direct (with filter) | Through autotransformer | Always via rectifier + inverter |
| Transfer time | 4–8 ms | 2–4 ms | **0 ms** |
| Output voltage regulation | Poor (depends on mains) | ±5 % (via tap-changer) | **±1 %** (active regulation) |
| Frequency stability | Same as mains | Same as mains | Internally crystal-locked |
| Surge protection | Limited | Good | **Excellent** (full isolation) |
| Efficiency at full load | **96–98 %** | 95–97 % | 90–94 % |
| Cost (per kVA) | Lowest | Medium | **Highest** |
| Typical use | PC, home router | Workstation, ATM, NAS | Server farm, hospital, fab |
| Noise (audible) | Quiet (fan off) | Moderate | High (fans always on) |
| Heat dissipation | Low | Medium | High (double conversion loss) |
