# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are written in **exhaustive, KTU-valuation-friendly** format. Every algebraic step is shown. Use the `[Eq. N]` tag references for easy recall during the exam.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Battery Backup Time of a UPS

**Given:** A single-phase online UPS feeds a critical load of **$P_{load} = 2\,\text{kW}$** at a power factor of **0.8 lagging**. The battery bank has a nominal voltage of **$V_{batt} = 96\,\text{V}$ DC** with a capacity of **$C_{Ah} = 150\,\text{Ah}$**. The overall system efficiency (inverter + transformer + filter losses) is **$\eta = 0.88$**.

**Find:** The expected backup time $T_{backup}$ in minutes.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the fundamental energy conservation equation for the UPS.**

The chemical energy stored in the battery (deliverable to the load) is reduced by the inefficiencies of the inverter, isolation transformer, and output filter. Hence:

$$E_{batt,usable} = E_{batt,stored} \times \eta_{overall}$$

**Step 2 — Express the stored energy of the battery bank in Wh.**

The DC energy stored in a battery of voltage $V_{batt}$ and capacity $C_{Ah}$ over a discharge time $T_{backup}$ is:

$$E_{batt,stored} = V_{batt} \times C_{Ah} = 96\,\text{V} \times 150\,\text{Ah}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerical value of stored energy.**

$$E_{batt,stored} = 14400\,\text{Wh} = 14.4\,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 4 — Express the load energy demand for the backup duration.**

The AC load consumes $P_{load}$ for $T_{backup}$ hours:

$$E_{load} = P_{load} \times T_{backup} = 2000\,\text{W} \times T_{backup}$$

**Step 5 — Equate usable battery energy to load demand (energy balance).**

$$V_{batt} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta_{overall} = P_{load} \times T_{backup}$$

**Step 6 — Isolate $T_{backup}$ on one side of the equation.**

$$T_{backup} = \dfrac{V_{batt} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta_{overall}}{P_{load}}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$T_{backup} = \dfrac{96 \times 150 \times 0.88}{2000}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the numerator first.**

$$96 \times 150 = 14400$$
$$14400 \times 0.88 = 12672$$

**Step 9 — Compute the final ratio.**

$$T_{backup} = \dfrac{12672}{2000} = 6.336\,\text{h}$$

**Step 10 — Convert hours to minutes for the final answer.**

$$T_{backup} = 6.336 \times 60 = 380.16\,\text{minutes} \approx 6\,\text{h}\,20\,\text{min}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Answer Statement (KTU Board Pattern):**
> *The UPS will provide a backup time of approximately 6 hours and 20 minutes for the given 2 kW load at 0.8 p.f., assuming the battery discharges linearly to 100 % DoD. In practice, the usable capacity is derated to 80 % DoD, giving a real backup of about **5 hours 4 minutes**.*

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Constant Current Charging Time

**Given:** A 12 V, 100 Ah VRLA battery is to be charged at the **C/10 rate** (i.e., 10 % of capacity per hour) from a fully discharged state (SOC = 0 %) to 100 % SOC, assuming a Coulombic efficiency of $\eta_C = 0.90$.

**Find:** The total charging time and the energy supplied by the charger.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the constant current magnitude.**

The C/10 rate for a 100 Ah battery is:

$$I_{charge} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{10} = \dfrac{100}{10} = 10\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Recall the Coulombic efficiency definition.**

Coulombic efficiency accounts for the fact that not all charge pushed in becomes stored — some is lost to gassing and heat:

$$\eta_C = \dfrac{Q_{stored}}{Q_{supplied}} = \dfrac{I_{charge} \times t_{effective}}{I_{charge} \times t_{actual}}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore, the actual charging time is longer than the ideal time.**

$$t_{actual} = \dfrac{t_{ideal}}{\eta_C} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{charge} \times \eta_C}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$t_{actual} = \dfrac{100}{10 \times 0.90} = \dfrac{100}{9} = 11.11\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the energy delivered by the charger (input side).**

The charger maintains an average terminal voltage slightly above the nominal — typically **$V_{avg} = 13.8\,\text{V}$** for a 12 V VRLA battery during the bulk phase:

$$E_{input} = V_{avg} \times I_{charge} \times t_{actual} = 13.8 \times 10 \times 11.11$$

**Step 6 — Compute the numerical value.**

$$E_{input} = 13.8 \times 111.1 = 1533.18\,\text{Wh} \approx 1.53\,\text{kWh}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the energy actually stored in the battery.**

$$E_{stored} = E_{input} \times \eta_C = 1533.18 \times 0.90 = 1379.86\,\text{Wh}$$

**Step 8 — Verify against the battery's rated energy.**

$$E_{rated} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah} = 12 \times 100 = 1200\,\text{Wh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Theoretical Note:** The "stored" energy of 1379 Wh is greater than the rated 1200 Wh because the terminal voltage during charging (13.8 V avg) is *higher* than the nominal 12 V. The *usable* energy at the terminals during discharge is still 1200 Wh.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Charging Current for a 3-Stage Charger (IUoU Profile)

**Given:** A 48 V VRLA battery bank (24 cells × 2 V) follows the **IUoU charging profile** (I = constant current, Uo = constant voltage, U = float). Specifications:
- Bulk charge current: $I_{bulk} = 0.2\,C$ (i.e., 20 % of rated Ah)
- Boost voltage: $V_{boost} = 2.40 \times 24 = 57.6\,\text{V}$
- Float voltage: $V_{float} = 2.25 \times 24 = 54.0\,\text{V}$
- Battery capacity: $C = 200\,\text{Ah}$

**Find:** The bulk-stage current, the boost duration, and the float-stage current at steady state.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Compute the bulk charging current.**

$$I_{bulk} = 0.2 \times C = 0.2 \times 200 = 40\,\text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the three stages of the IUoU profile.**

**Stage 1 (Bulk / I-stage):** Charger is a **current source** delivering $I_{bulk} = 40\,\text{A}$ into the battery. The terminal voltage rises gradually from $V_{cutoff}$ to $V_{boost}$. This stage restores approximately **70–80 %** of the capacity.

**Stage 2 (Absorption / Uo-stage):** Charger switches to a **voltage source** holding $V_{boost} = 57.6\,\text{V}$. The current *exponentially decays* as the battery's internal EMF approaches the boost setpoint. This stage restores the remaining **20–30 %** of capacity.

**Stage 3 (Float / U-stage):** Charger holds $V_{float} = 54.0\,\text{V}$ indefinitely. The current tapers to a small **trickle value** $I_{trickle}$ that just offsets the self-discharge.

**Step 3 — Estimate the bulk stage duration.**

The bulk stage delivers ~75 % of the Ah back. Charge restored:

$$Q_{bulk} = 0.75 \times C = 0.75 \times 200 = 150\,\text{Ah}$$

Time:

$$t_{bulk} = \dfrac{Q_{bulk}}{I_{bulk}} = \dfrac{150}{40} = 3.75\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 4 — Estimate the absorption (boost) stage duration.**

The remaining 25 % of capacity (50 Ah) is restored during the absorption stage. The current decays exponentially:

$$i(t) = I_{bulk} \times e^{-t/\tau_{bat}}$$

where $\tau_{bat}$ is the battery time constant (typically 1–2 h for VRLA). The boost stage typically takes 2–3 hours. Conservatively:

$$t_{boost} \approx 2.5\,\text{hours}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the steady-state float current.**

At float, the battery internal self-discharge current is balanced by the charger. For a 200 Ah VRLA cell, the self-discharge rate is ~3 %/month, i.e.:

$$I_{self-discharge} = \dfrac{0.03 \times 200}{30 \times 24} = 0.00833\,\text{A}$$

The float current must slightly exceed this. Practical float current:

$$I_{float} \approx 0.5\,\text{A to 2\,\text{A}}$$

**Step 6 — Final charging profile summary.**

| Stage | Mode | Setpoint | Duration | SOC reached |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk (I) | CC | $I = 40\,\text{A}$ | 3.75 h | 75 % |
| Absorption (Uo) | CV | $V = 57.6\,\text{V}$ | 2.5 h | 100 % |
| Float (U) | CV | $V = 54.0\,\text{V}$ | Indefinite | 100 % (held) |

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — DC-Bus Voltage and Hold-up Capacitor Sizing

**Given:** A 5 kVA, single-phase online UPS with output **$V_{out,rms} = 230\,\text{V}$** and switching frequency **$f_{sw} = 20\,\text{kHz}$**. During a 10 ms mains sag, the DC-bus voltage must not droop below **$V_{DC,min} = 350\,\text{V}$**.

**Find:** The required hold-up capacitor $C_{bulk}$ on the DC bus.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Establish the relationship between DC-bus voltage and AC output voltage.**

For a sinusoidal PWM inverter with modulation index $m_a \leq 1$:

$$V_{out,peak} = m_a \times \dfrac{V_{DC}}{2}$$

For a typical $m_a = 0.95$ and to deliver $V_{out,peak} = 230 \times \sqrt{2} = 325.3\,\text{V}$:

$$V_{DC,min} = \dfrac{2 \times V_{out,peak}}{m_a} = \dfrac{2 \times 325.3}{0.95} = 684.8\,\text{V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For this derivation, we accept the given $V_{DC,min} = 350\,\text{V}$ from the problem (representing a 110 V AC class UPS).

**Step 2 — Calculate the DC load current drawn by the inverter.**

The 5 kVA UPS at full load draws:

$$I_{DC} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{V_{DC,min}} = \dfrac{5000}{350} = 14.29\,\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the capacitor energy-discharge equation.**

The DC-bus capacitor supplies the load during the 10 ms mains sag. The voltage must not fall below the minimum:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} C \left(V_{DC,initial}^2 - V_{DC,min}^2 \right) = P_{out} \times \Delta t$$

**Step 4 — Assume initial DC-bus voltage.**

A regulated DC bus typically holds at $V_{DC,initial} = 400\,\text{V}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} C \left(400^2 - 350^2\right) = 5000 \times 0.010$$

**Step 5 — Compute the voltage-squared difference.**

$$400^2 = 160000$$
$$350^2 = 122500$$
$$160000 - 122500 = 37500$$

**Step 6 — Compute the right-hand side.**

$$5000 \times 0.010 = 50\,\text{J}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for C.**

$$\dfrac{1}{2} C \times 37500 = 50$$

$$C = \dfrac{2 \times 50}{37500} = \dfrac{100}{37500} = 2.667 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{F}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to microfarads.**

$$C = 2667\,\mu\text{F}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Practical Design Margin:** Engineers use a **2× safety factor** for electrolytic capacitor aging. So a **4700 μF, 500 V electrolytic capacitor** would be the actual choice, often implemented as a bank of parallel capacitors for current sharing.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams map the **functional architecture** of the three UPS topologies and the IUoU battery charging profile. They are rendered in a block-diagram style optimized for KTU board-exam sketches.

## 4.1 Online (Double Conversion) UPS — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[MAINS AC 230V 50Hz] -->|Input| nodeB[PFC Rectifier IGBT Boost]
    nodeB -->|DC Bus 400V| nodeC[DC Link Capacitor Cbulk]
    nodeC -->|DC Bus| nodeD[IGBT Inverter SPWM Full Bridge]
    nodeC <-->|Bidirectional Buck Boost| nodeE[Battery Bank 384V 100Ah]
    nodeD -->|PWM| nodeF[LC Output Filter]
    nodeF -->|Pure Sine| nodeG[Static Transfer Switch STS]
    nodeG -->|Critical Load| nodeH[Servers ICU PLC]
    nodeA -.->|Bypass Path| nodeG
    nodeI[Microcontroller DSP] -.->|Gate Signals| nodeB
    nodeI -.->|Gate Signals| nodeD
    nodeI -.->|Bidirectional Control| nodeE
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The thick arrows are the **power flow** (rectifier → DC bus → inverter → load).
- The dotted arrows are the **control signals** (DSP firing the IGBTs).
- The dashed line from MAINS to STS is the **bypass path** used during maintenance.
- The bidirectional block between DC bus and battery is the **bidirectional DC-DC converter** that handles both charging and discharging.

## 4.2 Offline (Passive Standby) UPS — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[MAINS AC] -->|Surge| nodeB[MOV Surge Suppressor]
    nodeB -->|Filtered| nodeC[EMI RFI LC Filter]
    nodeC -->|STS Pole 1| nodeD[Static Transfer Switch]
    nodeD -->|Output| nodeE[Critical Load]
    nodeA -->|Tapped| nodeF[Battery Charger SCR Rectifier]
    nodeF -->|DC| nodeG[VRLA Battery 12V Monobloc]
    nodeG -->|DC Link| nodeH[IGBT Inverter Cold Standby]
    nodeH -->|STS Pole 2| nodeD
    nodeI[Sensing and Control Circuit] -.->|Detect Mains Loss| nodeD
    nodeI -.->|Start Inverter| nodeH
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- In the *healthy mains* state, the STS connects Pole 1 → load. The inverter sits in cold standby.
- When the controller senses mains loss (under-voltage relay), the STS switches to Pole 2 within 4–8 ms.

## 4.3 Line-Interactive UPS — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[MAINS AC] -->|Input| nodeB[EMI Filter]
    nodeB -->|Filtered Mains| nodeC[Bidirectional Autotransformer with IGBT Tap Switcher]
    nodeC -->|Buck or Boost Regulated AC| nodeD[Static Transfer Switch]
    nodeD -->|Output| nodeE[Critical Load]
    nodeA -->|Charging Path| nodeF[SMPS Battery Charger]
    nodeF -->|DC| nodeG[VRLA Battery]
    nodeG -->|DC| nodeH[IGFT Inverter Active Parallel]
    nodeH -->|Compensation| nodeC
    nodeI[DSP Controller] -.->|Tap Selection| nodeC
    nodeI -.->|Reactive Compensation| nodeH
    nodeI -.->|Full Load on Mains Fail| nodeH
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The autotransformer performs **buck/boost voltage regulation** under DSP control.
- The inverter is in *active parallel* — it injects reactive power and corrects the power factor.
- On mains failure, the inverter transitions to *full-load mode* within 2–4 ms.

## 4.4 IUoU Battery Charging Profile — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BulkStage: SOC less than 75 percent
    BulkStage: BULK STAGE Constant Current 40A
    BulkStage --> AbsorptionStage: Vterm reaches 57.6V
    AbsorptionStage: ABSORPTION STAGE Constant Voltage 57.6V
    AbsorptionStage --> FloatStage: Icharge drops below 5A
    FloatStage: FLOAT STAGE Constant Voltage 54.0V
    FloatStage --> BulkStage: Mains restored after deep discharge
    FloatStage --> [*]: System OFF or Battery removed
```

**Reading the State Machine:**
- The charger **always enters the BULK stage** first when a deeply discharged battery is connected.
- The transition to ABSORPTION occurs when the terminal voltage hits the boost setpoint.
- The transition to FLOAT occurs when the charging current tapers below a threshold (typically 3–5 % of $C$).
- FLOAT is the *steady state* for an idling battery.

## 4.5 Pulse Charging Profile — Timing Diagram (Block Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Deeply Discharged Battery SOC 20%] -->|Initiate| nodeB[Pulse Train 100Hz 10 percent Duty]
    nodeB -->|T ON 1ms| nodeC[Charging Pulse Ipeak 3C]
    nodeC -->|T OFF 9ms| nodeD[Rest Period No Current]
    nodeD -->|Repeat 100 times| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Every 10th cycle| nodeE[Discharge Pulse Id 1C 1ms]
    nodeE -->|Desulfation| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Terminal Voltage Reaches Vmax| nodeF[Switch to CV Mode]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Pulse charging uses high-peak short-duration current pulses followed by rest periods.
- The discharge pulse (also called *reflex* pulse) depolarizes the battery and breaks up PbSO₄ crystals.
- After the battery reaches its voltage ceiling, the charger transitions to **constant voltage** mode.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are modeled strictly on **KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination (ESE)** patterns: 3-mark Part A questions and 14-mark Part B questions with **internal choice** (either-or pattern). Each question is tagged with a simulated past-year reference and mapped to a KTU Course Outcome (CO).

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, CO4, K3 — Apply]**
**List any three differences between Offline (VFD) and Online (VFI) UPS systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per valid difference):**

| S. No. | Offline (VFD) UPS | Online (VFI) UPS |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load is normally fed directly from the filtered mains | Load is always fed through the rectifier–inverter chain |
| 2 | Transfer time on mains failure is 4–8 ms (noticeable break) | Transfer time is effectively 0 ms (no break) |
| 3 | Lower cost and higher efficiency (~96–98 %) at full load | Higher cost and lower efficiency (~90–94 %) due to double conversion |
| 4 | Output voltage and frequency are *dependent* on mains | Output voltage and frequency are *independent* of mains |
| 5 | No galvanic isolation between input and output (unless transformer added) | Built-in galvanic isolation via the inverter transformer |
| 6 | Limited surge protection — only MOV at input | Full surge, sag, and noise isolation by PFC rectifier |

*(Any three of the above earn 3 marks. One mark per valid comparison pair.)*

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, CO4, K2 — Understand]**
**What is meant by the "float voltage" of a VRLA battery? Why is it maintained?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Float voltage is the continuous, regulated DC voltage applied to a fully charged VRLA battery to keep it at **100 % state of charge (SOC)** while compensating for its self-discharge losses. For a single 2 V VRLA cell, the standard float voltage is **2.25 V/cell** at 25 °C (so a 12 V monobloc = 13.5 V, a 48 V bank = 54.0 V).
- **Reason 1 (1 mark):** Float charging counteracts the battery's natural self-discharge rate (3–20 %/month for VRLA), preventing gradual capacity loss while the battery is idling.
- **Reason 2 (1 mark):** Maintaining float voltage prevents both **sulfation** (which occurs at under-voltage) and **gassing/dry-out** (which occurs at over-voltage above 2.4 V/cell), thereby maximizing the battery's service life (200–500 cycles for VRLA).

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE)

### Question B-A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, CO4, K4/K5 — Analyze/Evaluate]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, explain the operation of a **Double-Conversion Online UPS**. Compare its performance with that of a **Line-Interactive UPS** in terms of transfer time, efficiency, and output voltage regulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 kVA, three-phase online UPS feeds a server room load of **8 kW** at **0.9 power factor lagging**. The battery bank has a nominal voltage of **384 V DC** and a capacity of **$C = 100\,\text{Ah}$**. The overall UPS efficiency is **92 %**.
Calculate:
  (i) The **backup time** of the UPS in minutes. **(4 marks)**
  (ii) The **minimum number of 12 V, 100 Ah VRLA monobloc batteries** to be connected in series to form the 384 V bank. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Online UPS Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
Mains AC → [PFC Rectifier] → [DC Link 400V] ↔ [Battery via Bidirectional Converter]
                                              ↓
                                       [IGBT Inverter SPWM] → [LC Filter] → [STS] → Load
                                              ↑
                                       [DSP Control Unit]
```

**Operation (2 marks):**
- Under normal conditions, the mains is rectified by the **PFC (Power Factor Corrected) boost rectifier**, producing a regulated **400 V DC bus**. The **IGBT inverter** continuously synthesizes a **pure 230 V, 50 Hz sine wave** using SPWM/SVPWM.
- The battery is **permanently floating** on the DC bus via a bidirectional DC-DC converter. The inverter is *always* feeding the load, so the **transfer time is zero**.
- On mains failure, the PFC stops drawing current; the bidirectional converter discharges the battery into the DC bus; the inverter continues without interruption. The load sees a **clean, regulated sine wave throughout**.

**Comparison (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Online (VFI) | Line-Interactive (VI) |
|---|---|---|
| Transfer time | 0 ms | 2–4 ms |
| Efficiency at full load | 90–94 % | 95–97 % |
| Output voltage regulation | ±1 % (active) | ±5 % (tap-changer) |
| Galvanic isolation | Yes (inverter transformer) | No (autotransformer) |
| THD of output | <3 % | 5–8 % |

**(b) — 7 marks**

**(b)(i) — 4 marks: Backup time**

Given: $P_{load} = 8\,\text{kW}$, $V_{batt} = 384\,\text{V}$, $C = 100\,\text{Ah}$, $\eta = 0.92$.

$$T_{backup} = \dfrac{V_{batt} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta}{P_{load}}$$

$$T_{backup} = \dfrac{384 \times 100 \times 0.92}{8000} = \dfrac{35328}{8000} = 4.416\,\text{h}$$

Converting to minutes:

$$T_{backup} = 4.416 \times 60 = 264.96\,\text{minutes} \approx 4\,\text{h}\,25\,\text{min}$$

**Answer:** The UPS provides approximately **4 hours and 25 minutes** of backup at full load.

**(b)(ii) — 3 marks: Number of batteries**

A 12 V monobloc battery = 6 cells × 2 V = 12 V nominal.
To achieve 384 V:
$$N_{series} = \dfrac{384}{12} = 32\,\text{monoblocs}$$

**Answer:** **32 monobloc batteries** (each 12 V, 100 Ah) connected in **series** form the 384 V, 100 Ah battery bank.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, CO4, K3/K4 — Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram the **three-stage IUoU charging profile** of a VRLA battery. State the voltage and current setpoints for each stage for a 48 V, 200 Ah battery bank. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **expression for the backup time of a UPS** in terms of battery voltage, Ah capacity, load power, and overall efficiency. A 5 kW load is fed by a 240 V, 150 Ah battery bank with an overall UPS efficiency of 90 %. Calculate the backup time. If the charger is rated at the **C/5 rate** and the Coulombic efficiency is 0.85, calculate the **charging time** from 20 % SOC to 100 % SOC. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — 7 marks**

**Three-Stage IUoU Profile (5 marks for explanation + 2 marks for numerical setpoints):**

**Stage 1 — BULK (Constant Current / I-stage):**
- The charger acts as a **current source** delivering a fixed $I_{bulk} = 0.2\,C = 40\,\text{A}$ for the 200 Ah bank.
- The battery terminal voltage rises gradually from $V_{cutoff} = 1.75 \times 24 = 42.0\,\text{V}$ to $V_{boost} = 2.40 \times 24 = 57.6\,\text{V}$.
- This stage restores **~75 %** of the discharged capacity.
- Duration: ~3.75 hours at 0.2C rate.

**Stage 2 — ABSORPTION (Constant Voltage / Uo-stage):**
- The charger switches to a **voltage source** holding $V_{boost} = 57.6\,\text{V}$.
- The charging current **exponentially decays** as the battery's internal EMF approaches the boost setpoint.
- This stage restores the remaining **~25 %** of capacity.
- Duration: ~2.5 hours.
- Termination: $I_{charge} < 0.03 \times C = 6\,\text{A}$.

**Stage 3 — FLOAT (Constant Voltage / U-stage):**
- The charger holds $V_{float} = 2.25 \times 24 = 54.0\,\text{V}$ indefinitely.
- A small **trickle current** (~0.5–2 A) compensates for self-discharge.
- Battery is held at 100 % SOC for an unlimited time without damage.

**Setpoint Summary Table (2 marks):**

| Stage | Voltage Setpoint | Current Behavior | Duration |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bulk | Rising to 57.6 V | Constant 40 A | 3.75 h |
| Absorption | Constant 57.6 V | Decaying 40 A → 6 A | 2.5 h |
| Float | Constant 54.0 V | Trickle ~1 A | Indefinite |

**(b) — 7 marks**

**Derivation (3 marks):**
The energy balance for the UPS during battery discharge:

$$E_{batt,usable} = E_{load}$$
$$V_{batt} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta_{overall} = P_{load} \times T_{backup}$$
$$T_{backup} = \dfrac{V_{batt} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta_{overall}}{P_{load}} \quad \text{[Eq. 1]}$$

**Backup Time Calculation (2 marks):**
Given: $P_{load} = 5000\,\text{W}$, $V_{batt} = 240\,\text{V}$, $C_{Ah} = 150\,\text{Ah}$, $\eta = 0.90$.

$$T_{backup} = \dfrac{240 \times 150 \times 0.90}{5000} = \dfrac{32400}{5000} = 6.48\,\text{h} = 6\,\text{h}\,29\,\text{min}$$

**Charging Time Calculation (2 marks):**
Given: C/5 rate → $I_{charge} = 150/5 = 30\,\text{A}$, $\eta_C = 0.85$, charging from 20 % to 100 % SOC.
Ah to be restored: $\Delta Q = 0.80 \times 150 = 120\,\text{Ah}$.

$$t_{charge} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{I_{charge} \times \eta_C} = \dfrac{120}{30 \times 0.85} = \dfrac{120}{25.5} = 4.706\,\text{h} \approx 4\,\text{h}\,42\,\text{min}$$

**Answer:** Backup time = **6 h 29 min**; Charging time = **4 h 42 min**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on this Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "C" rating with Ah:** "Charging at C/10" means 10 % of Ah per hour, NOT 10 A. Always convert $C/10 = 0.1 \times C_{Ah}$.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert hours → minutes** in backup-time problems. Board answers must always end in minutes if the question asks for runtime in minutes.
> 3. **Confusing float voltage per cell (2.25 V) with nominal cell voltage (2.0 V).** Float is the *boosted* holding voltage, not the resting voltage.
> 4. **Mixing up series and parallel battery connections:** Series increases *voltage*; parallel increases *Ah capacity*. For a 384 V bank from 12 V monoblocs, the answer is **series** = 32. For higher Ah, you add *parallel strings*.
> 5. **Omitting the bidirectional DC-DC converter** in the online UPS block diagram. Examiners specifically look for this — without it, the battery cannot be both charged (rectifier → battery) and discharged (battery → DC bus → inverter) on the same physical port.
> 6. **Stating "transfer time = 0" for offline UPS** — a classic blunder. Offline has 4–8 ms; *only* Online has effectively 0 ms.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Last Hour of Exam Prep**

- **UPS = Uninterruptible Power Supply**, a battery-backed power-electronics system that provides clean, continuous AC to critical loads during mains failure or disturbance.
- **Three types as per IEC 62040-3:**
  - **VFD (Offline)** — load on raw mains, transfer 4–8 ms.
  - **VI (Line-Interactive)** — autotransformer regulates voltage, transfer 2–4 ms.
  - **VFI (Online Double Conversion)** — always through rectifier+inverter, transfer 0 ms.
- **Online UPS block path:** Mains → PFC Rectifier → DC Bus (~400 V) ↔ Battery (via bidirectional converter) → IGBT Inverter → LC Filter → STS → Load.
- **Battery chemistry in UPS:** Mostly **VRLA (2 V/cell, float 2.25 V, boost 2.40 V, cutoff 1.75 V)**; modern premium UPS use **LiFePO₄ (3.2 V/cell)**.
- **Charging methods:** CC, CV, Trickle, Pulse. Industrial UPS use the **IUoU three-stage profile** (Bulk → Absorption → Float).
- **Bulk stage:** Constant current at 0.1C–0.3C; voltage rises from 1.75 V/cell to 2.40 V/cell.
- **Absorption stage:** Constant voltage at 2.40 V/cell; current tapers; restores last 20–30 % of capacity.
- **Float stage:** Constant voltage at 2.25 V/cell; trickle current ~0.5–2 A; indefinite hold at 100 % SOC.
- **Backup time formula:** $T_{backup} = \dfrac{V_{batt} \times C_{Ah} \times \eta_{overall}}{P_{load}}$.
- **Charger current formula:** $I_{charge} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{T_{charge}} \times \dfrac{1}{\eta_C}$.
- **DC-bus voltage sizing:** $V_{DC} \geq \sqrt{2} \times V_{out,rms} / m_a$ (with $m_a \leq 1$).
- **Hold-up capacitor:** $C_{bulk} = \dfrac{2 \times P_{out} \times \Delta t}{V_{DC,initial}^2 - V_{DC,min}^2}$.
- **Series-parallel battery rules:** Series = +Voltage (V); Parallel = +Capacity (Ah).
- **Pulse charging benefit:** Reduces sulfation, allows higher average charge rate, lower heat.
- **Key efficiency rule:** Online UPS loses ~6–10 % more than offline due to double conversion; designers must oversize the cooling system.
- **KTU board favourites:**
  - "Explain online UPS with block diagram" (7 marks)
  - "Compare three UPS topologies" (7 marks)
  - "Calculate backup time / battery sizing" (7 marks)
  - "Explain IUoU charging profile" (7 marks)
- **Final mantra:** *"Offline is fast and cheap, Line-Interactive is balanced, Online is bulletproof."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recent Developments in Power Electronics for Automation and Robotics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT636 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Recent Developments in Power Electronics for Automation and Robotics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Recent Developments in Power Electronics for Automation and Robotics** refers to the modern paradigm shift in converter topologies, semiconductor devices, digital control architectures, and electromagnetic energy-transfer techniques that enable high-efficiency, high-power-density, and intelligent electrical energy conversion specifically tailored for **automated industrial systems, robotic manipulators, autonomous guided vehicles (AGVs), collaborative robots (cobots), and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs)**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic encompasses the convergence of **wide-bandgap (WBG) semiconductors**, **soft-switching resonant converters**, **modular multilevel converters (MMCs)**, **AI-enabled vector control**, **sensorless AC drives**, **wireless power transfer (WPT)**, and **digital twin emulation** as applied to motion-control and robotic actuation systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus Area:** The syllabus explicitly demands awareness of *why conventional silicon (Si) IGBT-based converters are being replaced*, and *how emerging technologies improve the torque response, switching frequency, reliability, and efficiency* of robotic actuators.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **human nervous system controlling muscles** in a robotic arm:

| Biological Analog | Power Electronics Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Brain | DSP / FPGA / Microcontroller | Decision making, modulation |
| Motor neurons | Gate drivers + IGBTs/SiC MOSFETs | Command transmission |
| Muscle fibers | BLDC / PMSM / SRM motors | Mechanical work output |
| Tendons (flexible) | Soft-switching snubbers | Smooth, low-loss motion |
| Skin sensors | Encoders, resolvers, Hall sensors | Feedback for closed-loop control |
| Wireless charging pad | Inductive WPT coil | Cable-free energy refilling |

In older systems, the "muscles" (motors) received "shock-commands" from bulky silicon switches — slow, lossy, and hot. **Recent developments** give the robot *faster reflexes (SiC/GaN at 100s of kHz)*, *smarter decisions (AI-tuned FOC)*, and *wireless energy* — making the machine lighter, faster, cooler, and more autonomous.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Referenced in this Module:**
> - Bandgap of Silicon (Si): **1.12 eV**
> - Bandgap of Silicon Carbide (SiC): **3.26 eV** (≈ 3× Si)
> - Bandgap of Gallium Nitride (GaN): **3.45 eV**
> - Critical electric field of Si: **0.3 MV/cm**
> - Critical electric field of SiC: **3.0 MV/cm** (≈ 10× Si)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Pillars of Modern Power Electronics for Automation

1. **Wide-Bandgap (WBG) Semiconductor Devices** — SiC MOSFETs, GaN HEMTs replacing Si IGBTs.
2. **Soft-Switching Resonant Topologies** — Zero-Voltage Switching (ZVS) / Zero-Current Switching (ZCS) eliminating switching losses.
3. **Modular Multilevel Converters (MMC)** — Scalable, harmonic-free medium-voltage drives.
4. **AI/ML-Driven Control** — Model Predictive Control (MPC), neural-network-based sensorless FOC.
5. **Wireless Power Transfer (WPT)** — Inductive/capacitive contactless charging for AGVs, AMRs.
6. **Integrated Motor Drives (IMD)** — Converter embedded inside the motor housing.

### 2.2 Pillar 1 — Wide-Bandgap Devices: The "Why"

A semiconductor's **Baliga's Figure of Merit (BFOM)** quantifies conduction loss reduction potential:

$$\text{BFOM} = \varepsilon_r \cdot \mu \cdot E_g^{\,3}$$

where $\varepsilon_r$ is relative permittivity, $\mu$ is carrier mobility, and $E_g$ is bandgap energy. Because $E_g$ is **cubed**, SiC and GaN deliver **orders-of-magnitude lower conduction losses** than Si — making them ideal for high-frequency robotic servo drives.

### 2.3 Pillar 2 — Soft-Switching Loss Reduction

Hard-switching loss per transition:

$$P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{DC} \cdot I_{load} \cdot (t_{on} + t_{off}) \cdot f_{sw}$$

Under **ZVS**, voltage across the device is ~ 0 at turn-on, so:

$$P_{sw,\text{ZVS}} \approx 0 \quad \text{(at turn-on)}$$

Result: switching frequency can be pushed from **20 kHz (Si IGBT)** → **500 kHz – 1 MHz (GaN)**, shrinking magnetics by 5×–10×.

### 2.4 Pillar 3 — Modular Multilevel Converter (MMC) for Robotic Cells

Each sub-module (SM) is a half-bridge with capacitor $C_{SM}$. The output is the sum of $N$ inserted capacitor voltages:

$$v_{out}(t) = \sum_{k=1}^{N} s_k(t) \cdot v_{C,k}(t)$$

where $s_k(t) \in \{0,1\}$ is the insertion index of the $k^{th}$ SM. This produces a near-sinusoidal waveform with **very low $dV/dt$**, protecting motor winding insulation in long-cable automation setups.

### 2.5 Pillar 4 — AI-Enhanced Field-Oriented Control (FOC)

Classical FOC uses PI regulators tuned by trial. **AI-driven FOC** replaces the PI loop with a regression model (e.g., LSTM, Random Forest, or TinyML model on edge-MCU) predicting the $d$-axis current $i_d^*(k+1)$ from torque demand and rotor speed $\omega_r(k)$:

$$i_d^*(k+1) = f_{\theta}\!\left(\, T^{*}(k),\ \omega_r(k),\ i_q(k),\ \theta_e(k)\,\right)$$

where $\theta$ are the learned network weights and $f_{\theta}$ is the inference function executed in < 20 µs on modern Arm Cortex-M7/M85 MCUs.

### 2.6 Pillar 5 — Wireless Power Transfer (WPT)

For a loosely-coupled inductive link with mutual inductance $M$:

$$P_{transfer} = \omega \cdot M \cdot I_p \cdot I_s \cdot \cos\phi$$

For an AGV docked over a charging pad, $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the operating frequency (typically **85 kHz** per SAE J2954 standard), and $M$ depends on coil alignment tolerance (~ 0.1–0.4 of peak $M_{max}$).

### 2.7 Pillar 6 — Integrated Motor Drives (IMD)

IMDs merge the inverter PCB **directly onto the motor's end-cap**. The thermal path is shortened, EMI loops are minimized, and cable-borne overvoltages are eliminated. Junction-to-ambient resistance:

$$\theta_{ja} = \theta_{jc} + \theta_{ch} + \theta_{ha}$$

is minimized to < **5 °C/W** in IMD designs vs. **15–25 °C/W** in discrete drives.

---

### 2.8 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Cheat-Sheet Table)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Engineering Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Baliga Figure of Merit | $\text{BFOM} = \varepsilon_r \mu E_g^{3}$ | Material-level conduction loss ranking | dimensionless |
| 2 | Hard-switching loss | $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_{load} (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$ | Switching loss per cycle | W |
| 3 | ZVS turn-on loss | $P_{sw} \approx 0$ at $v_{ds}\!=\!0$ | Lossless turn-on condition | W |
| 4 | Inductive WPT power | $P = \omega M I_p I_s \cos\phi$ | Power transferred across air gap | W |
| 5 | MMC output voltage | $v_{out} = \sum_{k=1}^{N} s_k v_{C,k}$ | Stacked sub-module synthesis | V |
| 6 | Induced EMF (PMSM) | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi \, k_w$ | Per-phase back EMF constant | V |
| 7 | Electromagnetic torque | $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2}\, p\, \psi_m\, i_q$ | Torque per q-axis current | N·m |
| 8 | Thermal resistance | $\theta_{ja} = \theta_{jc}+\theta_{ch}+\theta_{ha}$ | Junction-to-ambient heat path | °C/W |
| 9 | Resonant tank (LLC) | $f_r = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_r C_r}}$ | ZVS operating frequency | Hz |
| 10 | d-q transformation | $i_d = I \cos\theta,\ i_q = I \sin\theta$ | Field-oriented decoupling | A |
| 11 | Switching frequency ratio | $f_{sw,\text{GaN}}/f_{sw,\text{Si}} \approx 10\text{–}50$ | WBG speed-up factor | — |
| 12 | Efficiency upper bound | $\eta = P_{out}/(P_{out}+P_{loss})$ | Drive chain efficiency | % |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Switching Loss Reduction with ZVS

We derive the hard-switching loss expression and then show how ZVS eliminates it.

**Step 1 — Energy lost during turn-on overlap.** During the voltage–current crossover interval $\Delta t$, instantaneous power is $p(t) = v(t)\,i(t)$. Assuming linear overlap:

$$v(t) = \frac{V_{DC}}{\Delta t_{on}}\,t, \quad i(t) = \frac{I_{load}}{\Delta t_{on}}\,t$$

**Step 2 — Integrate to obtain energy per turn-on event.**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{on} &= \int_{0}^{\Delta t_{on}} v(t)\,i(t)\, dt \\[4pt]
       &= \int_{0}^{\Delta t_{on}} \frac{V_{DC}}{\Delta t_{on}}\,t \;\cdot\; \frac{I_{load}}{\Delta t_{on}}\,t \; dt \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{V_{DC}\,I_{load}}{(\Delta t_{on})^{2}} \int_{0}^{\Delta t_{on}} t^{2}\, dt \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{V_{DC}\,I_{load}}{(\Delta t_{on})^{2}} \cdot \frac{(\Delta t_{on})^{3}}{3} \\[4pt]
       &= \frac{1}{3}\, V_{DC}\,I_{load}\,\Delta t_{on}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Similarly for turn-off (energy stored in device output capacitance $C_{oss}$ discharged into stray inductance):**

$$E_{off} = \tfrac{1}{2}\, V_{DC}\,I_{load}\,\Delta t_{off}$$

**Step 4 — Total switching loss (per second, summed over $f_{sw}$ cycles):**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{sw} &= (E_{on} + E_{off}) \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
       &= \left[\tfrac{1}{3} V_{DC} I_{load} \Delta t_{on} + \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_{load} \Delta t_{off}\right] \cdot f_{sw} \\[4pt]
       &\approx \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_{load} (\Delta t_{on} + \Delta t_{off})\, f_{sw}
\end{aligned}$$

This recovers the standard KTU formula.

**Step 5 — Apply ZVS condition:** when the resonant inductor forces $v_{ds}(t)$ to ring down to **0 before** the gate signal is applied, $\Delta t_{on}$ becomes the duration of a *zero-voltage* transition. The energy dissipated in the channel becomes:

$$E_{on}^{\text{ZVS}} = \int_{0}^{\Delta t_{on}} v(t)\,i(t)\, dt = 0 \cdot (\text{something}) = 0$$

since $v(t) \equiv 0$ throughout. Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,P_{sw}^{\text{ZVS}} \approx \tfrac{1}{2}\, C_{oss}\,V_{DC}^{2}\, f_{sw} \ll P_{sw}^{\text{hard}}\,}$$

The residual loss is only the **capacitive charging loss**, which is often < 1 % of the original.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Electromagnetic Torque of a PMSM (used in Robotic Joints)

**Step 1 — Per-phase stator flux linkage** in a sinusoidal PMSM:

$$\psi_{ph}(\theta_e) = \psi_m \cos\theta_e$$

**Step 2 — Induced EMF** (Faraday's law):

$$e_{ph}(t) = -\dfrac{d\psi_{ph}}{dt} = \psi_m \,\omega_e\, \sin\theta_e$$

**Step 3 — Air-gap power** (3-phase balanced):

$$P_{gap} = 3\cdot\dfrac{e_{ph,\,rms}\cdot I_{ph,\,rms}}{...} = \tfrac{3}{2}\,\omega_e\,\psi_m\, i_q$$

**Step 4 — Convert mechanical power $T_e\,\omega_m$ to electrical $P_{gap}$:**

$$T_e\,\omega_m = \tfrac{3}{2}\,\omega_e\,\psi_m\, i_q,\quad \text{with } \omega_e = p\,\omega_m$$

**Step 5 — Solve for torque:**

$$\boxed{\,T_e = \tfrac{3}{2}\, p\, \psi_m\, i_q\,}$$

This is the foundational equation for robotic servo sizing — torque is *directly* proportional to the $q$-axis current $i_q$, enabling instantaneous torque control via FOC.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resonant Frequency of LLC Converter (WPT & EV Chargers)

The LLC tank has three reactive elements: $L_r$ (resonant inductor), $L_m$ (magnetizing), $C_r$ (resonant capacitor). The series resonant frequency is:

$$f_r = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_r C_r}}$$

The second (parallel) resonant point, which sets the boundary between ZVS and ZCS regions:

$$f_p = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{(L_r+L_m)\, C_r}}$$

Designers always operate the converter **between** $f_p$ and $f_r$ to guarantee ZVS on primary MOSFETs (critical for GaN-based automation chargers).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Switching Devices

| Parameter | Si IGBT | Si MOSFET | SiC MOSFET | GaN HEMT |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bandgap (eV) | 1.12 | 1.12 | 3.26 | 3.45 |
| Max $V_{DS}$ (V) | 1200–6500 | 40–600 | 650–1700 | 40–650 |
| $R_{DS(on)}$ at rated $V$ | High | Med | Low | Very Low |
| Switching freq. practical (kHz) | 5–20 | 50–200 | 100–500 | 200–1000 |
| Efficiency @ 50 % load | 92–94 % | 95–96 % | 97–98 % | 98–99 % |
| Cost / ampere | Lowest | Low | High | High |
| Robotic Use Case | Old CNC drives | Small servos | AGVs, cobots | UAVs, surgical robots |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: AI-Driven Robotic Drive System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Cloud MES / SCADA Planner]:::cloud -->|Task Command| B[Edge AI Controller Cortex-M85]:::ctrl
    B -->|Torque Ref T*| C[Model Predictive Controller MPC]:::mpc
    C -->|d-q Voltage Ref| D[SVPWM Modulator]:::mod
    D -->|Gate Pulses PWM1..PWM6| E[GaN Three-Phase Inverter 100V/30A]:::inv
    E -->|3-Phase AC| F[PMSM Robotic Joint 6-DoF]:::motor
    F -->|Shaft Motion| G[Encoder Resolver 23-bit]:::sens
    G -->|Position Speed| B
    F -->|Mechanical Link| H[Robot Arm End Effector]:::mech
    I[Battery Pack 48V Li-ion]:::bat -->|DC Bus| E
    J[Inductive WPT Pad 85 kHz]:::wpt -->|Contactless Charging| I
    K[Digital Twin GPU Server]:::twin <-->|Telemetry Sync 100Hz| B

    classDef cloud fill:#e8f4ff,stroke:#3b6ea5,color:#0b2545
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff7d6,stroke:#b48a00,color:#3d2c00
    classDef mpc fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#b30000,color:#3d0000
    classDef mod fill:#e8ffe8,stroke:#1a7a1a,color:#003300
    classDef inv fill:#f0e8ff,stroke:#5a2a8a,color:#2a0040

```

**Interpretation of Diagram:**
- The **Cloud MES** issues high-level job commands (e.g., "pick object A, place at B").
- The **Edge AI Controller** runs TinyML inference for collision avoidance and dynamic trajectory planning in real-time.
- **MPC + SVPWM** block generates optimized PWM within a 20 µs control loop.
- **GaN inverter** switches at up to 500 kHz with ZVS — silent (no acoustic noise from inverter-induced torque ripple).
- **WPT pad** recharges the battery when the AGV parks over the floor-mounted coil — no human intervention, no connector wear.
- **Digital Twin** continuously mirrors the robot's electrical/mechanical state for predictive maintenance.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Define Wide-Bandgap (WBG) semiconductors. List two advantages of SiC MOSFETs over Si IGBTs in robotic drives.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Wide-Bandgap semiconductors are power devices made from materials whose bandgap energy $E_g > 3 \text{ eV}$ (compared to Si at **1.12 eV**). Examples: Silicon Carbide (SiC, $E_g = 3.26$ eV) and Gallium Nitride (GaN, $E_g = 3.45$ eV).
>
> **Advantages (2 marks):**
> 1. **Higher switching frequency:** SiC MOSFETs can switch at **100–500 kHz** versus Si IGBT's 5–20 kHz, allowing smaller magnetic components and faster dynamic response in servo loops.
> 2. **Higher breakdown voltage & lower $R_{DS(on)}$:** Critical electric field of SiC is **10× that of Si**, enabling thinner drift regions and **lower conduction losses**, reducing heat-sink size in space-constrained robotic joints.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is Wireless Power Transfer (WPT)? State its relevance in mobile-robot automation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Wireless Power Transfer (WPT) is the transmission of electrical energy from a source to a load **without physical conductive contact**, typically via **mutual inductive coupling** at high frequency (e.g., 85 kHz per SAE J2954 standard), governed by $P_{transfer} = \omega M I_p I_s \cos\phi$.
>
> **Relevance (1.5 marks):**
> - Eliminates **connector wear** in AGVs that dock hundreds of times daily.
> - Enables **opportunity charging** at workstations → smaller battery pack → higher payload.
> - Permits **hermetically sealed robots** (e.g., cleanroom, medical) to recharge without exposed contacts.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an **AI-enhanced Field-Oriented Control (FOC)** system for a PMSM-driven robotic joint. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the electromagnetic torque expression $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2}\, p\, \psi_m\, i_q$ for a 3-phase PMSM. Explain why controlling $i_q$ directly gives "instantaneous torque" response. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(a)

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
The system consists of:
1. **Speed / Position Outer Loop** — receives $\omega_r^*$ from trajectory planner, generates $T_e^*$ via PI regulator.
2. **AI Inference Block** — replaces classical PI with a trained neural network or MPC solver running on edge-MCU; outputs $i_d^*, i_q^*$.
3. **Current Inner Loop** — Park / Clarke transforms convert 3-phase currents $i_a, i_b, i_c$ into $i_d, i_q$.
4. **Inverse Park + SVPWM** — generates 6 PWM signals to drive the 3-phase inverter.
5. **PMSM + Encoder** — physical plant with real-time feedback.

**Functional Explanation (4 marks):**
- **Why AI?** Classical PI requires manual tuning per motor variant; AI models adapt across **temperature, aging, load disturbance**.
- **Why $d$-axis current ≈ 0?** For surface-mounted PMSM, $i_d^* = 0$ maximizes torque per ampere, reducing copper loss.
- **Why $q$-axis current = torque?** Because $T_e \propto i_q$, controlling $i_q$ produces linear, decoupled torque — the holy grail for robotic joint precision.
- **Latency budget:** Total loop must execute in < 50 µs; modern GaN-driven FOC achieves this at **20 kHz control rate**.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.A(b)

**Step-by-step derivation (5 marks):** [Re-derived as in §3.2]

**Instantaneous Torque Explanation (2 marks):**
Because $T_e$ depends *only* on instantaneous $i_q$ (and constant machine parameters $p, \psi_m$), any change in $i_q$ reference is reflected as **immediate proportional mechanical torque**. There is no lag from flux dynamics (as in DC machines where field inductance slows response). This makes PMSMs the **gold standard** for robotic actuators needing **< 1 ms torque step response**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of Zero-Voltage Switching (ZVS)**. Derive the expression for hard-switching loss and show how ZVS reduces it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Modular Multilevel Converter (MMC)** topology. With a circuit diagram, explain its operation and list **three advantages** for medium-voltage robotic drive systems. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(a)

**ZVS Principle (3 marks):**
ZVS is a soft-switching technique where the **voltage across the switch is forced to zero before the gate is turned on**, using a resonant inductor or transformer leakage to circulate current and discharge the device output capacitance $C_{oss}$. The MOSFET then turns on at $v_{ds} \approx 0$, eliminating the $V \cdot I$ crossover loss.

**Hard-Switching Loss Derivation (3 marks):** [As derived in §3.1, Steps 1–4]

**ZVS Loss Reduction (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{\,P_{sw}^{\text{ZVS}} = \tfrac{1}{2} C_{oss} V_{DC}^{2} f_{sw}\,}$$

which is typically **< 5 %** of hard-switching loss. Net benefit: switching frequency $f_{sw}$ can be increased 5×–10× without thermal runaway.

---

#### Model Solution — Q.B(b)

**MMC Topology (3 marks):**
An MMC consists of **N series-connected sub-modules per arm**, each containing a half-bridge (or full-bridge) and a floating capacitor $C_{SM}$. Each phase-leg has an upper and lower arm, each with $N$ SMs and a buffer inductor $L_{arm}$. The synthesized AC output is:

$$v_{aN} = \tfrac{V_{DC}}{2} - \sum_{k=1}^{N} s_{u,k}\, v_{C,u,k}$$

**Operation (2 marks):**
Each SM is either **inserted** ($s_k = 1$) or **bypassed** ($s_k = 0$). By coordinating all SMs, the converter produces a stepped waveform approximating a sinusoid with very low harmonic distortion (THD < 1 % without filters).

**Three Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Scalability:** Add sub-modules for higher voltage levels (e.g., 6.6 kV robotic CNC drives).
2. **Low $dV/dt$:** Protects motor winding insulation in long-cable installations.
3. **Redundancy:** Failed sub-modules can be bypassed → graceful degradation, critical for 24/7 factory automation.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "soft switching" with "snubber circuits"** — Snubbers *absorb* hard-switching losses into resistors; soft switching *eliminates* the overlap altogether. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Writing only the formula for torque** $T_e$ **without showing the $d$-axis decoupling** — students forget that $\psi_d = L_d i_d + \psi_m$ and assume $\psi_d = \psi_m$, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Omitting the $C_{oss}$ residual loss term** in ZVS derivations — examiners want you to show that ZVS is *not lossless*, only *near-lossless*. Loss of 1 mark if omitted.
> 4. **Drawing MMC with a single capacitor per phase** instead of N sub-modules — 2-mark deduction; the entire concept of modularity is missed.
> 5. **Citing non-KTU-specific references** (IEEE papers from 2015, outdated datasheets) — use 2020+ standards (SAE J2954 for WPT, IEC 61800 for drives).

---

- ✅ **WBG semiconductors** (SiC, GaN) have bandgap > 3 eV → faster switching, lower loss.

- ✅ **Hard-switching loss** $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} V_{DC} I_{load} (t_{on}+t_{off}) f_{sw}$.
- ✅ **ZVS** eliminates turn-on crossover loss; residual = $\tfrac{1}{2} C_{oss} V_{DC}^{2} f_{sw}$.
- ✅ **PMSM torque** $T_e = \tfrac{3}{2} p\, \psi_m\, i_q$ — controlling $i_q$ = direct torque.
- ✅ **FOC** decouples flux ($i_d$) and torque ($i_q$) via Park transform.
- ✅ **MMC** stacks N sub-modules per arm → near-sinusoidal output, scalable voltage.
- ✅ **WPT** operates at **85 kHz** (SAE J2954) for AGV charging; power $\propto \omega M$.
- ✅ **AI-MPC + TinyML** on edge MCUs replaces fixed-gain PI in modern drives.
- ✅ **Integrated Motor Drives (IMD)** minimize $\theta_{ja}$ and eliminate cable reflections.
- ✅ **Digital Twins** enable predictive maintenance, reduce downtime by up to 60 %.
